clause.title: Copyright
clause.p: Even if electronic versions are available free of charge on the website of one or more of the JCGM member organizations, economic and moral copyrights related to all JCGM publications are internationally protected. The JCGM does not, without its written authorisation, permit third parties to rewrite or re-brand issues, to sell copies to the public, or to broadcast or use on-line its publications. Equally, the JCGM also objects to distortion, augmentation or mutilation of its publications, including its titles, slogans and logos, and those of its member organizations.

clause.title: Official versions and translations
clause.p: The only official versions of documents are those published by the JCGM, in their original languages.

The JCGM’s publications may be translated into languages other than those in which the documents were originally published by the JCGM. Permission must be obtained from the JCGM before a translation can be made. All translations should respect the original and official format of the formulae and units (without any conversion to other formulae or units), and contain the following statement (to be translated into the chosen language):

The JCGM retains full internationally protected copyright on the design and content of this document and on the JCGM’s titles, slogan and logos. The member organizations of the JCGM also retain full internationally protected right on their titles, slogans and logos included in the JCGM’s publications. The only official version is the document published by the JCGM, in the original languages.

clause.p: The JCGM does not accept any liability for the relevance, accuracy, completeness or quality of the information and materials offered in any translation. A copy of the translation shall be provided to the JCGM at the time of publication.

clause.title: Reproduction
clause.p: The JCGM’s publications may be reproduced, provided written permission has been granted by the JCGM. A sample of any reproduced document shall be provided to the JCGM at the time of reproduction and contain the following statement:A

clause.p: The JCGM and its member organizations have published this document to enhance access to information about metrology. They endeavor to update it on a regular basis, but cannot guarantee the accuracy at all times and shall not be responsible for any direct or indirect damage that may result from its use. Any reference to commercial products of any kind (including but not restricted to any software, data or hardware) or links to websites, over which the JCGM and its member organizations have no control and for which they assume no responsibility, does not imply any approval, endorsement or recommendation by the JCGM and its member organizations.

foreword.title: Foreword
foreword.p: In 1997 a Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology ( JCGM), chaired by the Director of the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM), was created by the seven international organizations that had originally in 1993 prepared the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” (GUM) and the “International vocabulary of basic and general terms in metrology” (VIM). The JCGM assumed responsibility for these two documents from the ISO Technical Advisory Group 4 (TAG4).

The Joint Committee is formed by the BIPM with the International Electrotechnical Commission ( IEC), the International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine ( IFCC), the International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation ( ILAC), the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry ( IUPAC), the International Union of Pure and Applied Physics ( IUPAP), and the International Organization of Legal Metrology ( OIML).

JCGM has two Working Groups. Working Group 1, “Expression of uncertainty in measurement”, has the task to promote the use of the GUM and to prepare Supplements and other documents for its broad application. Working Group 2, “Working Group on International vocabulary of basic and general terms in metrology (VIM)”, has the task to revise and promote the use of the VIM.

Supplements such as this one are intended to give added value to the GUM by providing guidance on aspects of uncertainty evaluation that are not explicitly treated in the GUM. The guidance will, however, be as consistent as possible with the general probabilistic basis of the GUM.

The present Supplement 2 to the GUM has been prepared by Working Group 1 of the JCGM, and has benefited from detailed reviews undertaken by member organizations of the JCGM and National Metrology Institutes.

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.p: The “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” (GUM) [ ] is mainly concerned with univariate measurement models, namely models having a single scalar output quantity. However, models with more than one output quantity arise across metrology. The GUM includes examples, from electrical metrology, with three output quantities [ H.2], and thermal metrology, with two output quantities [ H.3]. This Supplement to the GUM treats multivariate measurement models, namely models with any number of output quantities. Such quantities are generally mutually correlated because they depend on common input quantities. A generalization of the GUM uncertainty framework [ 5] is used to provide estimates of the output quantities, the standard uncertainties associated with the estimates, and covariances associated with pairs of estimates. The input or output quantities in the measurement model may be real or complex.

Supplement 1 to the GUM [] is concerned with the propagation of probability distributions [ 5] through a measurement model as a basis for the evaluation of measurement uncertainty, and its implementation by a Monte Carlo method [ 7]. Like the GUM, it is only concerned with models having a single scalar output quantity [ 1]. This Supplement describes a generalization of that Monte Carlo method to obtain a discrete representation of the joint probability distribution for the output quantities of a multivariate model. The discrete representation is then used to provide estimates of the output quantities, and standard uncertainties and covariances associated with those estimates. Appropriate use of the Monte Carlo method would be expected to provide valid results when the applicability of the GUM uncertainty framework is questionable, namely when (a) linearization of the model provides an inadequate representation, or (b) the probability distribution for the output quantity (or quantities) departs appreciably from a (multivariate) Gaussian distribution.

Guidance is also given on the determination of a coverage region for the output quantities of a multivariate model, the counterpart of a coverage interval for a single scalar output quantity, corresponding to a stipulated coverage probability. The guidance includes the provision of coverage regions that take the form of hyper-ellipsoids and hyper-rectangles. A calculation procedure that uses results provided by the Monte Carlo method is also described for obtaining an approximation to the smallest coverage region.

clause.title: Scopecovariance coverage probability coverage region coverage regionhyper-ellipsoidal coverage regionhyper-rectangular coverage regionsmallest estimate of input quantities Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement (GUM) GUM uncertainty framework Monte Carlo method (MCM) sensitivity coefficient standard uncertainty Supplement 1 to the GUM
clause.p: This Supplement to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” (GUM) is concerned with measurement models having any number of input quantities (as in the GUM and GUM Supplement 1) and any number of output quantities. The quantities involved might be real or complex. Two approaches are considered for treating such models. The first approach is a generalization of the GUM uncertainty framework. The second is a Monte Carlo method as an implementation of the propagation of distributions. Appropriate use of the Monte Carlo method would be expected to provide valid results when the applicability of the GUM uncertainty framework is questionable.

The approach based on the GUM uncertainty framework is applicable when the input quantities are summarized (as in the GUM) in terms of estimates (for instance, measured values) and standard uncertainties associated with these estimates and, when appropriate, covariances associated with pairs of these estimates. Formulae and procedures are provided for obtaining estimates of the output quantities and for evaluating the associated standard uncertainties and covariances. Variants of the formulae and procedures relate to models for which the output quantities (a) can be expressed directly in terms of the input quantities as measurement functions, and (b) are obtained through solving a measurement model, which links implicitly the input and output quantities.

The counterparts of the formulae in the GUM for the standard uncertainty associated with an estimate of the output quantity would be algebraically cumbersome. Such formulae are provided in a more compact form in terms of matrices and vectors, the elements of which contain variances (squared standard uncertainties), covariances and sensitivity coefficients. An advantage of this form of presentation is that these formulae can readily be implemented in the many computer languages and systems that support matrix algebra.

The Monte Carlo method is based on (i) the assignment of probability distributions to the input quantities in the measurement model [ 6], (ii) the determination of a discrete representation of the (joint) probability distribution for the output quantities, and (iii) the determination from this discrete representation of estimates of the output quantities and the evaluation of the associated standard uncertainties and covariances. This approach constitutes a generalization of the Monte Carlo method in Supplement 1 to the GUM, which applies to a single scalar output quantity.

For a prescribed coverage probability, this Supplement can be used to provide a coverage region for the output quantities of a multivariate model, the counterpart of a coverage interval for a single scalar output quantity. The provision of coverage regions includes those taking the form of a hyper-ellipsoid or a hyper-rectangle. These coverage regions are produced from the results of the two approaches described here. A procedure for providing an approximation to the smallest coverage region, obtained from results provided by the Monte Carlo method, is also given.
This Supplement contains detailed examples to illustrate the guidance provided.
This document is a Supplement to the GUM and is to be used in conjunction with it and GUM Supplement 1. The audience of this Supplement is that of the GUM and its Supplements. Also see .

terms.title: Terms and definitionsInternational vocabulary of metrology (VIM)
terms.p: For the purposes of this Supplement, the definitions of the GUM and the VIM apply unless otherwise indicated. Some of the most relevant definitions, adapted or generalized where necessary from these documents, are given below. Further definitions are given, including definitions taken or adapted from other sources, that are especially important for this Supplement.
A glossary of principal symbols used is given in .

verbal-definition.p: quantity whose numerical value is a real number
quantity whose numerical value is a complex number

stem.asciimath: Z -= (Z_r, Z_{theta})sf(T) = Z_r (cos Z_{theta} + "i" sin Z_{theta}) = Z_r e^{"i" Z_{theta}},

termnote.p: where Z r Z_r and Z θ Z_{theta} are, respectively, the magnitude (amplitude) and phase of Z bb(Z).

verbal-definition.p: set of quantities arranged as a matrix having a single column
vector quantity with real components

termexample.p: A real vector quantity X bb(X) containing N N real quantities X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N expressed as a matrix of dimension N × 1 N xx 1:

verbal-definition.p: vector quantity with complex components

termexample.p: A complex vector quantity Z bb(Z) containing N N complex quantities Z 1 , … , Z N bb(Z)_1, ..., bb(Z)_N expressed as a matrix of dimension N × 1 N xx 1:

verbal-definition.p: vector quantity intended to be measured

termnote.p: Generalized from 2.3.

verbal-definition.p: mathematical relation among all quantities known to be involved in a measurement

termnote.p: Adapted from 2.48.

A general form of a measurement model is the equation h ( Y , X 1 , … , X N ) = 0 h(Y, X_1, ..., X_N) = 0, where Y Y, the output quantity in the measurement model, is the measurand, the quantity value of which is to be inferred from information about input quantities X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N in the measurement model.
In cases where there are two or more output quantities in a measurement model, the measurement model consists of more than one equation.

verbal-definition.p: measurement model in which there is any number of output quantities

termnote.p: The general form of a multivariate measurement model is the equations

where Y 1 , … , Y m Y_1, ..., Y_m, the output quantities, m m in number, in the multivariate measurement model, constitute the measurand, the quantity values of which are to be inferred from information about input quantities X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N in the multivariate measurement model.
A vector representation of the general form of multivariate measurement model is
where Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T bb(Y) = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)} and h = ( h 1 , … , h m ) T bb(h) = (h_1, ..., h_m)^{sf(T)} are matrices of dimension m × 1 m xx 1.
If, in , m m, the number of output quantities, is unity, the model is known as a univariate measurement model.

verbal-definition.p: function in a multivariate measurement model for which the output quantities are expressed in terms of the input quantities

termnote.p: Generalized from 2.49.

If a measurement model h ( Y , X ) = 0 bb(h)(bb(Y), bb(X)) = 0 can explicitly be written as Y = f ( X ) bb(Y) = bb(f)(bb(X)), where X = ( X 1 , … , X N ) T bb(X) = (X_1, ..., X_N)^{sf(T)} are the input quantities, and Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T bb(Y) = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)} are the output quantities, f = ( f 1 , … , f m ) T bb(f) = (f_1, ..., f_m)^{sf(T)} is the multivariate measurement function. More generally, f bb(f) may symbolize an algorithm, yielding for input quantity values x = ( x 1 , … , x N ) T bb(x) = (x_1, ..., x_N)^{sf(T)} a corresponding unique set of output quantity values y 1 = f 1 ( x ) , … , y m = f m ( x ) y_1 = f_1(bb(x)), ..., y_m = f_m(bb(x)).
If, in note , m m, the number of output quantities, is unity, the function is known as a univariate measurement function.

verbal-definition.p: measurement model, generally multivariate, involving real quantities
measurement model, generally multivariate, involving complex quantities
measurement model, generally multivariate, consisting of a sequence of sub-models, in which output quantities from one sub-model become input quantities to a subsequent sub-model

termnote.p: Only at the final stage of a multistage measurement model might it be necessary to consider a coverage region for the output quantities based on the joint probability density function for those quantities.

termexample.p: A common instance in metrology is the following pair of measurement sub-models in the context of calibration. The first sub-model has input quantities whose measured values are provided by measurement standards and corresponding indication values, and as output quantities the parameters in a calibration function. This sub-model specifies the manner in which the output quantities are obtained from the input quantities, for example by solving a least-squares problem. The second sub-model has as input quantities the parameters in the calibration function and a quantity realized by a further indication value and as output quantity the quantity corresponding to that input quantity.

The covariance matrix of the random variable X bb(X) is V ( X ) bb(V(X)), a symmetric positive semi-definite matrix of dimension N × N N xx N containing the covariances Cov ( X i , X j ) "Cov"(X_i, X_j). Certain operations involving V ( X ) bb(V(X)) require positive definiteness.

verbal-definition.p: symmetric positive semi-definite matrix of dimension N × N N xx N associated with an estimate of a real vector quantity of dimension N × 1 N xx 1, containing on its diagonal the squares of the standard uncertainties associated with the respective components of the estimate of the quantity, and, in its off-diagonal positions, the covariances associated with pairs of components of that estimate

termnote.p: Adapted from definition 3.11.
A covariance matrix U x bb(U_x) of dimension N × N N xx N associated with the estimate x bb(x) of a quantity X bb(X) has the representation

where u ( ξ , ξ ) = u 2 ( ξ ) u(xi, xi) = u^2(xi) is the variance (squared standard uncertainty) associated with ξ xi and u ( ξ , x j ) u(xi, x_j) is the covariance associated with ξ xi and x j x_j. When elements X i X_i and X j X_j of X bb(X) are uncorrelated, u ( ξ , x j ) = 0 u(xi, x_j) = 0.
In GUM Supplement 1 [], the measurement covariance matrix is termed uncertainty matrix.

Some numerical difficulties can occasionally arise when working with covariance matrices. For instance, a covariance matrix U x bb(U_x) associated with a vector estimate x bb(x) may not be positive definite. That possibility can be a result of the way U x bb(U_x) has been calculated. As a consequence, the Cholesky factor of U x bb(U_x) may not exist. The Cholesky factor is used in working numerically with U x bb(U_x) ; also see . Moreover, the variance associated with a linear combination of the elements of x bb(x) could be negative, when otherwise it would be expected to be small and positive. In such a situation procedures exist for “repairing” U x bb(U_x) such that the repaired covariance matrix is positive definite. As a result, the Cholesky factor would exist, and variances of such linear combinations would be positive as expected. Such a procedure is given by the following variant of that in reference . Form the eigendecomposition

where Q bb(Q) is orthonormal and D bb(D) is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues of U x bb(U_x). Construct a new diagonal matrix, D ′ bb(D)' say, which equals D bb(D), but with elements that are smaller than d min d_{"min"} replaced by d min d_{"min"}. Here, d min d_{"min"} equals the product of the unit roundoff of the computer used and the largest element of D bb(D). Subsequent calculations would use a repaired covariance matrix U x ′ bb(U_x)' formed from
Certain operations involving U x bb(U_x) require positive definiteness.

verbal-definition.p: symmetric positive semi-definite matrix of dimension N × N N xx N associated with an estimate of a real vector quantity of dimension N × 1 N xx 1, containing the correlations associated with pairs of components of the estimate

termnote.p: A correlation matrix R x bb(bb(R_x)) of dimension N × N N xx N associated with the estimate x bb(x) of a quantity X bb(X) has the representation

stem.asciimath: bb(R_x) = [(r(x_1, x_1), cdots, r(x_1, x_N)),(vdots, ddots, vdots), (r(x_N, x_1), cdots, r(x_N, x_N))],

termnote.p: where r ( x i , x i ) = 1 r(x_i, x_i) = 1 and r ( x i , x j ) r(x_i, x_j) is the correlation associated with x i x_i and x j x_j. When elements X i X_i and X j X_j of X bb(X) are uncorrelated, r ( x i , x j ) = 0 r(x_i, x_j) = 0.
Correlations are also known as correlation coefficients.
R x bb(R_x) is related to U x bb(U_x) (see ) by
where D x D_x is a diagonal matrix of dimension N × N N xx N with diagonal elements u ( x 1 ) , … , u ( x N ) u(x_1), ..., u(x_N). Element ( i , j ) (i, j) of U x bb(U_x) is given by

stem.asciimath: u(x_i, x_j) = r(x_i, x_j) u(x_i) u(x_j) .

termnote.p: A correlation matrix R x bb(R_x) is positive definite or singular, if and only if the corresponding covariance matrix U x bb(U_x) is positive definite or singular, respectively. Certain operations involving R x bb(R_x) require positive definiteness.

When presenting numerical values of the off-diagonal elements of a correlation matrix, rounding to three places of decimals is often sufficient. However, if the correlation matrix is close to being singular, more decimal digits need to be retained in order to avoid numerical difficulties when using the correlation matrix as input to an uncertainty evaluation. The number of decimal digits to be retained depends on the nature of the subsequent calculation, but as a guide can be taken as the number of decimal digits needed to represent the smallest eigenvalue of the correlation matrix with two significant decimal digits. For a correlation matrix of dimension 2 × 2 2 xx 2, the eigenvalues λ max lambda_{"max"} and λ min lambda_{"min"} are 1 − | r | 1 - |r|, the smaller, λ min lambda_{"min"}, being 1 − | r | 1 - |r|, where r r is the off-diagonal element of the matrix. If a correlation matrix is known to be singular prior to rounding, rounding towards zero reduces the risk that the rounded matrix is not positive semi-definite.

verbal-definition.p: matrix of partial derivatives of first order for a real measurement model with respect to either the input quantities or the output quantities evaluated at estimates of those quantities

termnote.p: For N N input quantities and m m output quantities, the sensitivity matrix with respect to X bb(X) has dimension m × N m xx N and that with respect to Y bb(Y) has dimension m × m m xx m.

verbal-definition.p: interval containing the true quantity value with a stated probability, based on the information available

termnote.p: Adapted from 3.12.

The probabilistically symmetric coverage interval for a scalar quantity is the interval such that the probability that the true quantity value is less than the smallest value in the interval is equal to the probability that the true quantity value is greater than the largest value in the interval [adapted from 3.15].
The shortest coverage interval for a quantity is the interval of shortest length among all coverage intervals for that quantity having the same coverage probability [adapted from 3.16].

verbal-definition.p: region containing the true vector quantity value with a stated probability, based on the information available
probability that the true quantity value is contained within a specified coverage interval or coverage region

termnote.p: Adapted from 3.13.
The coverage probability is sometimes termed “level of confidence” [ 6.2.2].

verbal-definition.p: coverage region for a vector quantity with minimum (hyper-)volume among all coverage regions for that quantity having the same coverage probability

termnote.p: For a single scalar quantity, the smallest coverage region is the shortest coverage interval for the quantity. For a bivariate quantity, it is the coverage region with the smallest area among all coverage regions for that quantity having the same coverage probability.

verbal-definition.p: probability distribution of a random variable X bb(X) of dimension N × 1 N xx 1 having the joint probability density function

stem.asciimath: g_{bb(X)}(bb(xi)) = 1/{(2pi)^{N//2} ["det"(bb V)]^{1//2}} exp(-1/2 (bb xi - mu)^{sf(T)} bb(V)^{-1} (bb xi - bb mu))

termnote.p: μ bb mu is the expectation and V bb V is the covariance matrix of X bb X, which must be positive definite.

verbal-definition.p: probability distribution of a random variable X bb X of dimension N × 1 N xx 1 having the joint probability density function

stem.asciimath: g_{bb(X)}(bb(xi)) = {Gamma ({nu + N}/2)}/{Gamma (nu/2)(pi nu)^{N//2}} xx 1/{["det"(bb V)]^{1//2}} [1 + 1/nu (bb xi - bb mu)^{sf(T)} bb V^{-1} (bb xi - bb mu)]^{-(nu + N)//2},

verbal-definition.p: with parameters μ bb mu, V bb V and ν nu, where V bb V is symmetric positive definite and
is the gamma function

termnote.p: The multivariate t-distribution is based on the observation that if a vector random variable Q bb Q of dimension N × 1 N xx 1 and a scalar random variable W W are independent and have respectively a Gaussian distribution with zero expectation and positive definite covariance matrix V bb V of dimension N × N N xx N, and a chi-squared distribution with ν nu degrees of freedom, and ( ν / W ) 1 / 2 Q = X − μ (nu//W)^{1//2} bb Q = bb X - bb mu, then X bb X has the given probability distribution.

g X ( ξ ) g_{bb(X)}(bb(xi)) does not factorize into the product of N N probability density functions even when V bb V is a diagonal matrix. Generally, the components of X bb X are statistically dependent random variables.

termexample.p: When N = 2 N = 2, ν = 5 nu = 5, and V bb V is the identity matrix of dimension 2 × 2 2 xx 2, the probability that X 1 > 1 X_1 > 1 is 18 %, while the conditional probability that X 1 > 1 X_1 > 1 given that X 2 > 2 X_2 > 2 is 26 %.

clause.title: Conventions and notation
clause.p: For the purposes of this Supplement the following conventions and notation are adopted.

index.primary: Type A and Type B uncertainty evaluation

clause.p: In the GUM [4.1.11], for economy of notation the same (upper case) symbol is used for

li.p: the (physical) quantity, which is assumed to have an essentially unique true value, and
the corresponding random variable.

note.p: The random variable has different roles in Type A and Type B uncertainty evaluations. In a Type A uncertainty evaluation, the random variable represents “…​the possible outcome of an observation of the quantity”. In a Type B uncertainty evaluation, the probability distribution for the random variable describes the state of knowledge about the quantity.

clause.p: This ambiguity in the symbol is harmless in most circumstances.
In this Supplement, as well as in Supplement 1, for the input quantities subjected to a Type A uncertainty evaluation, the same (uppercase) symbol is used for three concepts, namely

li.p: the quantity,
the random variable representing the possible outcome of an observation of the quantity, and
the random variable whose probability distribution describes the state of knowledge about the quantity.

clause.p: This further ambiguity between two distinct random variables is not present in the GUM and is a potential source of misunderstanding. In particular, it might be believed that the Monte Carlo procedure adopted in this Supplement, as well as in Supplement 1, complies with the procedure suggested in GUM 4.1.4 note. Although the two procedures are analogous in principle, in that both are based on repeatedly evaluating the model for individual input quantity values drawn from a probability distribution, they are different in practice, in that draws are made from different distributions. In GUM 4.1.4 note, draws are made from the frequency distribution for the random variable . In the Supplements, draws are made from the probability distribution for the random variable . The former approach is not recommended in most experimental situations .

The input quantities in a measurement model are generically denoted by X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N, and collectively by X = ( X 1 , … , X N ) T X = (X_1, ..., X_N)^{sf(T)}, a matrix of dimension N × 1 N xx 1, T sf(T) denoting “transpose”.

Likewise the output quantities in a measurement model are generically denoted by Y 1 , … , Y m Y_1, ..., Y_m, and collectively by Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T bb Y = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)}, a matrix of dimension m × 1 m xx 1.
When the Y i Y_i are expressed directly as formulae in X bb X, the measurement model can be written as
where f bb f is the multivariate measurement function. Equivalently (see ), the measurement model can be expressed as

stem.asciimath: Y_1 = f_1(bb X), ..., Y_m = f_m(bb X),

clause.p: where f 1 ( X ) , … , f m ( X ) f_1(bb X), ..., f_m(bb X) are components of f ( X ) bb f(bb X).
When the Y i Y_i are not expressed directly as formulae in X bb X, the measurement model is represented by the equation
or, equivalently (see ), by

stem.asciimath: h_1(bb Y, bb X) = 0, ..., h_m(bb Y,bb X) = 0 .

clause.p: An estimate of X bb X is denoted by x = ( x 1 , … , x N ) T bb x = (x_1, ..., x_N)^{sf(T)}, a matrix of dimension N × 1 N xx 1. The covariance matrix associated with x bb x is denoted by U x bb(U_x), a matrix of dimension N × N N xx N (see ).

An estimate of
Y
bb Y is denoted by
y
=
(
y 1
, … ,
y m
)
T
bb y = (y_1, ..., y_m)^{sf(T)}, a matrix of dimension
m × 1
m xx 1. The covariance matrix associated with
y
bb y is denoted by
U y
bb(U_y), a matrix of dimension
m × m
m xx m.
U y bb(U_y) is the counterpart, for m m output quantities, of the variance u 2 ( y ) u^2(y) associated with y y in the context of the univariate measurement models of the GUM and GUM Supplement 1. In the GUM, u ( y ) u(y) is denoted by u c ( y ) u_c(y), the subscript “c” denoting combined. As in Supplement 1, the use of “c” in this context is considered superfluous for the reasons stated in of that Supplement. Accordingly, “c” is similarly not used in this Supplement.

When estimates of the output quantities in a measurement model are to be used individually, each of these quantities may be considered as the output quantity in the corresponding univariate (scalar) measurement model. When the output quantities are to be considered together, for instance used in a subsequent calculation, any correlations associated with pairs of estimates of the output quantities need to be taken into account.

The symbol adopted for the standard uncertainty associated with a quantity value x x is u ( x ) u(x). When the context is such that there is no possibility of misunderstanding, the alternative notation u x u_x can be adopted. The alternative notation is not recommended when a quantity value is indexed or otherwise adorned, for example x i x_i or x ^ hat x.

x bb x may be described as either “estimates of the input quantities”, or “an estimate of the (vector) input quantity”. The latter description is mainly used in this Supplement (and similarly for the output quantities).

As in subclauses to , a quantity is generally denoted by an upper case letter and an estimate or some fixed value of the quantity (such as the expectation) by the corresponding lower case letter. Although valuable for generic considerations, such a notation is largely inappropriate for physical quantities, because of the established use of specific symbols, for example T T for thermodynamic temperature and t t for time. Therefore, in some of the examples, a different notation is used, in which a quantity is denoted by its conventional symbol and its expectation or an estimate of it by that symbol hatted. For instance, the quantity representing the amplitude of an alternating current ( ) is denoted by I I and an estimate of I I by I ^ hat I [4.8].

A departure is made from the symbols often used for a probability density function (PDF) and distribution function. The GUM uses the generic symbol f f to refer to a model and a PDF. Little confusion arises in the GUM as a consequence of this usage. The situation in this Supplement is different. The concepts of measurement function, PDF, and distribution function are central to following and implementing the guidance provided. Therefore, in place of the symbols f f and F to denote a PDF and a distribution function, respectively, the symbols g g and G G are used. These symbols are indexed appropriately to denote the quantity concerned. The symbol f f is reserved for a measurement function. Vector counterparts of these symbols are also used.

A PDF is assigned to a quantity, which might be a single scalar quantity X X or a vector quantity X bb X. In the scalar case, the PDF for X X is denoted by g X ( ξ ) g_{bb(X)}(bb(xi)), where ξ xi is a variable describing the possible values of X X. This X X is considered as a random variable with expectation E ( X ) E(X) and variance V ( X ) V(X).

In the vector case, the PDF for X bb X is denoted by g X ( ξ ) g_{bb(X)}(bb(xi)), where ξ = ( ξ 1 , … , ξ N ) T bb xi = (xi_1, ..., xi_N)^{sf(T)} is a variable describing the possible values of the quantity X bb X. This X bb X is considered as a random variable with expectation E ( X ) bb E(bb X) and covariance matrix V ( X ) bb V(bb X).
Analogously, in the scalar case, the PDF for Y Y is denoted by g Y ( η ) g_Y(eta) and, in the vector case, the PDF for Y bb Y is denoted by g Y ( η ) g_{bb Y} (bb eta).

According to Resolution 10 of the 22nd CGPM (2003) “…​the symbol for the decimal marker shall be either the point on the line or the comma on the line …​”. The JCGM has decided to adopt, in its documents in English, the point on the line.

In the GUM [4.1], a measuring system is modelled in terms of a function involving real input quantities X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N and a real output quantity Y Y in the form of , namely Y = f ( X ) Y = f(bb X), where X ≡ ( X 1 , … , X N ) T bb X -= (X_1, ..., X_N)^{sf(T)} is referred to as the real vector input quantity. This function is known as a real univariate measurement function (see ).
In practice, not all measuring systems encountered can be modelled as a measurement function in a single scalar output quantity. Such systems might instead involve either

li.p: a number of output quantities Y 1 , … , Y m Y_1, ..., Y_m (denoted collectively by the real vector output quantity Y ≡ ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T ) Y -= (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)}), taking the form , namely Y = f ( X ) bb Y = bb f(bb X), or
the more general form of measurement model, taking the form , namely h ( Y , X ) = 0 bb h(bb Y,bb X) = bb 0.

clause.p: Further, some or all of the components of X bb X and Y bb Y might correspond to components (real part and imaginary part, or magnitude and phase) of complex input quantities. Therefore, all measurement models can be considered (without loss of generality) as real. However, a treatment that is simpler than the corresponding treatment involving real and imaginary parts applies for the complex case . The treatment gives succinct matrix expressions for the law of propagation of uncertainty when applied to measurement models with complex quantities (complex models). See and .
This GUM Supplement considers the more general measurement models in and .

clause.title: Main stages of uncertainty evaluationstages of uncertainty evaluation

index.secondary: assigning a probability density function (PDF) to

clause.p: The main stages of uncertainty evaluation constitute formulation, propagation, and summarizing:

li.p: Formulation:
define the output quantity Y bb Y, the quantity intended to be measured (the vector measurand),
determine the input quantity X bb X upon which Y bb Y depends,
develop a measurement function [ f bb f in ] or measurement model relating X bb X and Y bb Y,

on the basis of available knowledge assign PDFs — Gaussian (normal), rectangular (uniform), etc. — to the components of X bb X. Assign instead a joint PDF to the components of X bb X that are not pairwise independent,
Propagation:
propagate the PDFs for the components of X bb X through the model to obtain the (joint) PDF for Y bb Y,
Summarizing:
use the PDF for Y bb Y to obtain
the expectation of Y bb Y, taken as an estimate y bb y of Y bb Y,
the covariance matrix of Y bb Y, taken as the covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) associated with y bb y, and
a coverage region containing Y Y with a specified probability p p (the coverage probability).

index.secondary: assigning a probability density function (PDF) to

clause.p:
The steps in the formulation stage are carried out by the metrologist. Guidance on the assignment of PDFs (step 4 of stage ) is given in GUM Supplement 1 for some common cases and in . The propagation and summarizing stages, and , for which detailed guidance is provided here, require no further metrological information, and in principle can be carried out to any required numerical tolerance for the problem specified in the formulation stage.
Once the formulation stage has been carried out, the PDF for Y bb Y is completely specified mathematically, but generally the calculation of the expectation, covariance matrix and coverage regions require numerical methods that involve a degree of approximation.

clause.title: Probability density functions for the input quantities
GeneralGaussian distributionmultivariate indication values input quantityassigning a probability density function (PDF) to t-distributionmultivariate
clause.p: GUM Supplement 1 gives guidance on the assignment, in some common circumstances, of PDFs to the input quantities X i X_i in the formulation stage of uncertainty evaluation [ 6]. The only multivariate distribution considered in GUM Supplement 1 is the multivariate Gaussian [ 6.4.8]. This distribution is assigned to the input quantity X bb X when the estimate x bb x and associated covariance matrix U x bb(U_x) constitute the only information available about X bb X. A further multivariate distribution, the multivariate t-distribution, is described in . This distribution arises when a series of indication values, regarded as being obtained independently from a vector quantity with unknown expectation and unknown covariance matrix having a multivariate Gaussian distribution, is the only information available about X bb X. Also see .

Suppose that a series of n n indication values x 1 , … , x n x_1, ..., x_n, each of dimension N × 1 N xx 1, is available, with n > N n > N, regarded as being obtained independently from a quantity with unknown expectation μ bb mu and covariance matrix ∑ sum of dimension N × N N xx N having the multivariate Gaussian distribution N ( μ , ∑ ) N(bb mu, bb sum). The desired input quantity X bb X of dimension N × 1 N xx 1 is taken to be equal to μ bb mu. Then, assigning a non-informative joint prior distribution to μ bb mu and ∑ bb sum, and using Bayes’ theorem, the marginal (joint) PDF for X bb X is a multivariate t-distribution t ν ( x ¯ , S / n ) t_nu(bb(bar x), bb S//n) with ν = n − N nu = n - N degrees of freedom , where

stem.asciimath: bar x = 1/n (x_1 + ... + x_n), " " " " bb(S) = 1/nu [(x_1 - bar x) (x_1 - bar x)^{sf T} + ... + (x_n - bar x)(x_n - bar x)^{sf T}].

note.p: The prior can be assigned in other ways, which would influence the degrees of freedom or even the distribution.

clause.p: The PDF for X bb X is
where Γ ( z ) Gamma(z) is the gamma function with argument z z.
X bb X has expectation and covariance matrix

stem.asciimath: bb E(bb X) = bar(bb x), " " " " bb V(bb X) = nu/{nu - 2} bb(S)/n,

clause.p: where E ( X ) bb E(bb X) is defined only for ν > 1 nu > 1 (that is for n > N + 1 n > N + 1) and V ( X ) bb V(bb X) only for ν > 2 nu > 2 (that is for n > N + 2 n > N+2).

To make a random draw from t ν ( x ¯ , S / n ) t_{nu}(bar(bb(x)),bb S//n), make N N random draws z i z_i, i = 1 , … , N i = 1, ..., N, from the standard Gaussian distribution N(0, 1) and a single random draw w w from X ν 2 sf(X)_{nu}^2, the chi-squared distribution with nu degrees of freedom, and form

stem.asciimath: bb (xi = bar bb(x) + L z) sqrt(nu/w), " " " " z = (z_1, ..., z_N)^{sf(T)},

clause.p:
where

N × N

The matrix L bb L can be determined as in reference , for example.

clause.title: Construction of multivariate probability density functionscopula
clause.p: When the input quantities X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N are correlated, the information that typically is available about them are the forms of their PDFs (say, that one is Gaussian, another is rectangular, etc.), estimates x 1 , … , x N x_1, ..., x_N, used as their expectations, associated standard uncertainties u ( x 1 ) , … , u ( x N ) u(x_1), ..., u(x_N), used as their standard deviations, and covariances associated with pairs of the x i x_i. A mathematical device known as a copula can be used to produce a PDF for X bb X consistent with such information. Such a PDF is not unique.

(left) shows an instance of a measurement model in which there are N = 3 N = 3 mutually independent input quantities X = ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 ) T bb X = (X_1, X_2, X_3)^{sf(T)} and m = 2 m = 2 output quantities Y = ( Y 1 , Y 2 ) T bb Y = (Y_1, Y_2)^{sf(T)}. The measurement function is f = ( f 1 , f 2 ) T bb f = (f_1, f_2)^{sf(T)}. For i = 1 , 2 , 3 i = 1, 2, 3, X i X_i is assigned the PDF g X i ( ξ i ) g_{X_i}(xi_i), and Y bb Y is characterized by the joint PDF g Y ( η ) ≡ g Y 1 , Y 2 ( η 1 , η 2 ) g_{bb Y}(eta) -= g_{Y_1, Y_2} (eta_1, eta_2). (right) adapts this instance to where X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 are mutually dependent and characterized by the joint PDF g X 1 , X 2 ( ξ 1 , ξ 2 ) g_{X_1,X_2} (xi_1, xi_2).

There might be an additional output quantity, Q bb Q say, depending on Y bb Y. Y bb Y would be regarded as an input quantity to a further measurement model represented in terms of a measurement function t bb t, say:
For example, Y bb Y might be the vector of masses in a set of mass standards, and Q bb Q the sums of some of them.

figure.name: Propagation of distributions for N = 3 N = 3 input quantities and m = 2 m = 2 output quantities when the input quantities X 1 , X 2 X_1, X_2 and X 3 X_3 are mutually independent and (right) when X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 are mutually dependent ()

clause.p: Composition of the measurement functions f bb f and t bb t, which are regarded as sub-models, gives Q bb Q as a function of X bb X. It can, however, be desirable to retain the individual sub-models when they relate to functionally distinct stages. These two sub-models constitute an instance of a multistage model (see ).

The case when the final stage of a multistage model has an output quantity that is a single scalar quantity, and that quantity is the only output quantity of interest, can be handled using GUM Supplement 1.
An estimate y bb y of the output quantity Y bb Y is the expectation E ( Y ) bb (E(Y)). The covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) associated with y bb y is the covariance matrix V ( Y ) bb(V(Y)).
For a coverage probability p p, a coverage region R Y R_{bb Y} for Y bb Y satisfies

stem.asciimath: int_{R_{bb Y}} g_{bb Y} (bb eta) "d" bb(eta) = p

note.p: Although random variables corresponding to some quantities might be characterized by distributions having no expectation or covariance matrix (see, for example, ), a coverage region for Y bb Y always exists.
Generally there is more than one coverage region for a stated coverage probability p p.

clause.p: There is no direct multivariate counterpart of the probabilistically symmetric 100 p % 100p% coverage interval considered in GUM Supplement 1. There is, however, a counterpart of the shortest 100 p % 100p% coverage interval. It is the 100 p % 100p % coverage region for Y bb Y of smallest hyper-volume.

clause.title: Implementations of the propagation of distributionspropagation of distributionsimplementations of

clause.p: The propagation of distributions can be implemented in several ways:

li.p: analytical methods, that is methods that provide a mathematical representation of the PDF for Y bb Y,

uncertainty propagation based on replacing the model by a first-order Taylor series approximation (a generalization of the treatment in the GUM [ 5.1.2]) — the (generalized) law of propagation of uncertainty,
numerical methods [G.1.5] that implement the propagation of distributions, specifically using a Monte Carlo method (MCM).

note.p: Solutions expressible analytically are ideal in that they do not introduce any approximation. They are applicable in simple cases only, however. These methods are not considered further in this Supplement, apart from in the examples for comparison purposes.

MCM as considered here is regarded as a means for providing a numerical representation of the distribution for the vector output quantity, rather than a simulation method per se. In the context of the propagation stage of uncertainty evaluation, the problem to be solved is deterministic, there being no random physical process to be simulated.

clause.p: In uncertainty propagation, the estimate x = E ( X ) bb(x = E(X)) of X bb X and the associated covariance matrix U x = V ( X ) bb(U_x) = bb(V(X)) are propagated through (a linearization of) the measurement model. This Supplement provides procedures for doing so for the various types of model considered.

(left) illustrates the (generalized) law of propagation of uncertainty for a measurement model with N = 3 N = 3 mutually independent input quantities X = ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 ) T bb X = (X_1, X_2, X_3)^{sf(T)} and m = 2 m = 2 output quantities Y = ( Y 1 , Y 2 ) T bb Y = (Y_1, Y_2)^{sf(T)}. X bb X is estimated by x = ( x 1 , x 2 , x 3 ) T bb x = (x_1, x_2, x_3)^{sf(T)} with associated standard uncertainties u ( x 1 ) u(x_1), u ( x 2 ) u(x_2) and u ( x 3 ) u(x_3). Y bb Y is estimated by y = ( y 1 , y 2 ) T bb y = (y_1, y_2)^{sf(T)} with associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y). (right) applies when X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 are mutually dependent with covariance u ( x 1 , x 2 ) u(x_1, x_2) associated with the estimates x 1 x_1 and x 2 x_2.

figure.name: Generalized law of propagation of uncertainty for N = 3 N = 3 mutually independent input quantities X 1 X_1, X 2 X_2 and X 3 X_3, and m = 2 m = 2 (almost invariably) mutually dependent output quantities, and (right) as left, but for mutually dependent X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 ()

clause.p: In MCM, a discrete representation of the (joint) probability distribution for X bb X is propagated through the measurement model to obtain a discrete representation of the (joint) probability distribution for Y bb Y from which the required summary information is determined.

The propagation of uncertainty for measurement models that are more general than the form Y = f ( X ) Y = f(bb X) in the GUM is described (see and ). Although such measurement models are not directly considered in the GUM, the same underlying principles may be used to propagate estimates of the input quantities and the uncertainties associated with the estimates through the measurement model to obtain estimates of the output quantities and the associated uncertainties. Mathematical expressions for the evaluation of uncertainty are stated using matrix-vector notation, rather than the subscripted summations given in the GUM, because generally such expressions are more compact and more naturally implemented within modern software packages and computer languages.
For the application of the law of propagation of uncertainty, the same information concerning the input quantities as for the univariate measurement model treated in the GUM is used:

li.p: an estimate x = ( x 1 , … , x N ) T bb x = (x_1, ..., x_N)^{sf(T)} of the input quantity X bb X,

the covariance matrix U x bb(U_x) associated with x bb x containing the covariances u ( ξ , x j ) u(xi, x_j), i = 1 , … , N i = 1, ..., N, j = 1 , … , N j = 1, ..., N, associated with x i x_i and x j x_j.

clause.p: The description of the propagation of uncertainty given in and is for real measurement models, including complex measurement models that are expressed in terms of real quantities. A treatment for complex measurement models is given in . Also see .
Obtaining a coverage region for a vector output quantity is described in .

clause.title: Propagation of uncertainty for explicit multivariate measurement modelsmeasurement modelexplicit multivariate propagation of uncertainty

clause.p: An explicit multivariate measurement model specifies a relationship between an output quantity Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T bb Y = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)} and an input quantity X = ( X 1 , … , X N ) T bb X = (X_1, ..., X_N)^{sf(T)}, and takes the form

where
f
bb f denotes the multivariate measurement function.
Any particular function f j ( X ) f_j(bb X) may depend only on a subset of X bb X, with each X i X_i appearing in at least one function.
Given an estimate x bb x of X bb X, an estimate of Y bb Y is
The covariance matrix of dimension m × m m xx m associated with y bb y is

stem.asciimath: bb(U_y) = [(u(y_1, y_1), cdots, u(y_1, y_m)), (vdots,ddots,vdots) (u(y_m, y_1), cdots, u(y_m, y_m))],

clause.p: where cov ( y j , y j ) = u 2 ( y j ) "cov"(y_j, y_j) = u^2(y_j), and is given by
where C x bb(C_x) is the sensitivity matrix of dimension m × N m xx N given by evaluating

clause.title: ExamplesGUM uncertainty frameworkexamples of measurement modelmultistage

example.name: Resistance and reactance of a circuit element [H.2]
example.p: The resistance R R and reactance X X of a circuit element are determined by measuring the amplitude V V of a sinusoidal alternating potential difference applied to it, the amplitude I I of the alternating current passed through it, and the phase angle ϕ phi between the two. The bivariate measurement model for R R and X X in terms of V V, I I and ϕ phi is

stem.asciimath: R = f_1(V, I, phi) = V/I cos phi, " " " " X = f_2(V, I, phi) = V/I sin phi .

example.p: In terms of the general notation, N = 3 N = 3, m = 2 m = 2, X ≡ ( V , I , ϕ ) T bb X -= (V, I, phi)^{sf(T)} and Y ≡ ( R , X ) T bb Y -= (R, X)^{sf(T)}.

An estimate y ≡ ( R ^ , X ^ ) T bb y -= (hat R, hat X)^{sf(T)} of resistance and reactance is obtained by evaluating expressions at an estimate x ≡ ( V ^ , I ^ , ϕ ^ ) T x -= (hat V, hat I, hat phi)^{sf(T)} of the input quantity X bb X.
The covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) of dimension 2 × 2 2 xx 2 associated with y bb y is given by , where C x bb(C_x) is the sensitivity matrix of dimension 2 × 3 2 xx 3 given by evaluating

stem.asciimath: [({del f_1}/{del V}, {del f_1}/{del I}, {del f_1}/{del phi}),({del f_2}/{del V}, {del f_2}/{del I}, {del f_2}/{del phi})] = [({cos phi}/I, -{V cos phi}/{I^2}, -{V sin phi}/I),({sin phi}/I, -{V sin phi}/{I^2}, -{V cos phi}/I)]

In the GUM, reactance is denoted by X X, which is the notation used here. The reactance X X, a component of the vector output quantity Y bb Y, is not to be confused with X bb X, the vector input quantity.

example.name: Reflection coefficient measured by a microwave reflectometer (approach 1)
example.p: The (complex) reflection coefficient Γ bb(Gamma) measured by a calibrated microwave reflectometer, such as an automatic network analyser, is given by the complex measurement model
where W bb W is the (complex) uncorrected reflection coefficient and a bb a, b bb b and c bb c are (complex) calibration coefficients characterizing the reflectometer , , .
In terms of the general notation, and working with real and imaginary parts of the quantities involved, N = 8 N = 8, m = 2 m = 2,

stem.asciimath: bb X -= (a_R, a_I, b_R, b_I, c_R, c_I, W_R, W_I)^{sf(T)}" " "and" " " bb Y -= (Gamma_R, Gamma_I)^{sf(T)}.

example.p: An estimate y ≡ ( Γ ^ R , Γ ^ I ) T bb y -= (hat(Gamma)_R, hat(Gamma)_I)^{sf(T)} of the (complex) reflection coefficient is given by the real and imaginary parts of the right-hand side of evaluated at the estimate x bb x of the input quantity X bb X.
The covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) of dimension 2 × 2 2 xx 2 associated with y bb y is given by , where C x bb(C_x) is the sensitivity matrix of dimension 2 × 8 2 xx 8 given by evaluating

stem.asciimath: [({del Gamma_R}/{del a_R}, {del Gamma_R}/{del a_I}, {del Gamma_R}/{del b_R}, {del Gamma_R}/{del b_I}, {del Gamma_R}/{del c_R}, {del Gamma_R}/{del c_I}, {del Gamma_R}/{del W_R}, {del Gamma_R}/{del W_I}), ({del Gamma_I}/{del a_R}, {del Gamma_I}/{del a_I}, {del Gamma_I}/{del b_R}, {del Gamma_I}/{del b_I}, {del Gamma_I}/{del c_R}, {del Gamma_I}/{del c_I}, {del Gamma_I}/{del W_R}, {del Gamma_I}/{del W_I})]

example.p: at X = x bb X = bb x, and U x bb(U_x) is the covariance matrix of dimension 8 × 8 8 xx 8 associated with x bb x.

example.name: Calibration of mass standards
example.p: This example constitutes an instance of a multistage model (see , and ).

A set of q q mass standards of unknown mass values m = ( m 1 , … , m q ) T bb m = (m_1, ..., m_q)^{sf(T)} is calibrated by comparison with a reference kilogram, using a mass comparator, a sensitivity weight for determining the comparator sensitivity, and a number of ancillary instruments such as a thermometer, a barometer and a hygrometer for determining the correction due to air buoyancy. The reference kilogram and the sensitivity weight have masses m R m_R and m S m_S, respectively. The calibration is carried out by performing, according to a suitable measurement procedure, a sufficient number k k of comparisons between groups of standards, yielding apparent, namely, in-air differences δ = ( δ 1 , … , δ k ) T bb delta = (delta_1, ..., delta_k)^{sf(T)}. Corresponding buoyancy corrections b = ( b 1 , … , b k ) T bb b = (b_1, ..., b_k)^{sf(T)} are calculated. In-vacuo mass differences X bb X are obtained from the sub-model X = f ( W ) bb(X = f (W)), where W = ( m R , m S , δ T , b T ) T bb W = (m_R, m_S, bb(delta)^{sf T}, bb(b)^{sf T})^{sf T}.

An estimate y ≡ ( m ^ 1 , … , m ^ q ) T bb y -= ( hat(m)_1, ..., hat(m)_q)^{sf(T)} of the masses m bb m is typically given by the least-squares solution of the over-determined system of equations A m = X bb(Am = X), where A bb A is a matrix of dimension k × q k xx q with elements equal to +1+1, -1-1 or zero, according to the mass standards involved in each comparison, and respecting the uncertainties associated with the estimate x bb x of X bb X. With this choice, the estimate y bb y is given by

stem.asciimath: bb(y) = bb(U_y A^{sf T} U_x x),

example.p: where the covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) of dimension q × q q xx q associated with y bb y is given by U y = ( A T U x − 1 A ) − 1 bb(U_y) = (bb(A^{sf T} U_x^{-1} A))^{-1} is the covariance matrix of dimension k × k k xx k associated with x bb x. A more detailed description of the sub-model, as well as a procedure for obtaining U x bb(U_x) in terms of U w bb(U_w), the covariance matrix associated with the estimate w bb w of W bb W, is available .
The multivariate measurement model for this example can be expressed as

stem.asciimath: bb Y = bb(U_y A^{sf T} U_x^{-1} X),

clause.title: Propagation of uncertainty for implicit multivariate measurement modelsmeasurement modelimplicit multivariate

clause.p: An implicit multivariate measurement model specifies a relationship between an output quantity Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T bb Y = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)} and an input quantity X = ( X 1 , … , X N ) T bb X = (X_1, ..., X_N)^{sf(T)}, and takes the form
Given an estimate x bb x of X bb X, an estimate y bb y of Y bb Y is given by the solution of the system of equations

note.p: The system of equations has generally to be solved numerically for y bb y, using, for example, Newton’s method or a variant of that method, starting from an approximation y ( 0 ) y^{(0)} to the solution.

clause.p: The covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) of dimension m × m m xx m associated with y bb y is evaluated from the system of equations

example.name: Set of pressures generated by a pressure balance
example.p: The pressure p p generated by a pressure balance is defined implicitly by the equation

stem.asciimath: p = {m_w (1 - rho_a//rho_w) g_{ell}}/{A_0 (1 + lambda_p)(1 + alpha delta theta)},

example.p: where m w m_w is the total applied mass, ρ a rho_a and ρ w rho_w are, respectively, the densities of air and the applied masses, g e ≪ g_{ell} is the local acceleration due to gravity, A 0 A_0 is the effective cross-sectional area of the balance at zero pressure, λ lambda is the distortion coefficient of the piston-cylinder assembly, α alpha is the coefficient of thermal expansion, and δ θ delta theta is the deviation from a 20 °C reference Celsius temperature .

Let p 1 , … , p q p_1, ..., p_q denote the generated pressures for, respectively, applied masses m w , 1 , … , m w , q m_{w, 1}, ..., m_{w, q} and temperature deviations δ θ 1 , … , δ θ q delta theta_1, ..., delta theta_q.

In terms of the general notation, N = 6 + 2 q N = 6 + 2q, m = q m = q, X ≡ ( A 0 , λ , α , δ θ 1 , m w , 1 , … , δ θ q , m w , q , ρ a , ρ w , g e ≪ ) T bb X -= (A_0, lambda, alpha, delta theta_1, m_{w, 1}, ..., delta theta_q, m_{w, q}, rho_a, rho_w, g_{ell})^{sf(T)} and Y ≡ ( p 1 , … , p q ) T Y -= (p_1, ..., p_q)^{sf(T)}.
X bb X and Y bb Y are related by the measurement model

stem.asciimath: h_j(bb Y,bb X) = A_0 p_j (1 + lambda p_j) (1 + alpha delta theta_j) - m_{w, j} (1 - rho_a//rho_w) g_{ell} = 0, " " " " j = 1, ..., q .

example.p: An estimate p ^ j hat(p)_j of p ^ j hat p_j is obtained by solving an equation of the form given estimates of A 0 A_0, λ lambda, α alpha, δ θ j delta theta_j, m w , j m_{w, j}, ρ a rho_a, ρ w rho_w and g e ≪ g_{ell}. However, the resulting estimates have associated covariances because they all depend on the measured quantities A 0 A_0, λ lambda, α alpha, ρ a rho_a, ρ w rho_w and g e ≪ g_{ell}.

A measurement function [giving Y j ( ≡ p j ) Y_j (-= p_j) explicitly as a function of X bb X] can be determined in this case as the solution of a quadratic equation. Such a form is not necessarily numerically stable. Moreover, measurement models involving additional, higher-order powers of p p are sometimes used . Determination of an explicit expression is not generally possible in such a case.
There is more than one way to express the measurement model . For instance, in place of the form , the model based on equating to zero the difference between the left- and right-hand sides of model could be used. The efficiency and stability of the numerical solution of the measurement model depends on the choice made.
More complete models of the pressure generated by a pressure balance can also be considered , which include, for example, a correction to account for surface tension effects.
Not all the input quantities appear in each equation, with the jth equation involving only A 0 , λ , α , δ θ j , m w , j , ρ a , ρ w A_0, lambda, alpha, delta theta_j, m_{w, j}, rho_a, rho_w and g e ≪ g_{ell}.

example.name: Reflection coefficient measured by a microwave reflectometer (approach 2)
example.p: Another approach to is to relate the input quantity X ≡ ( a R , a I , b R , b I , c R , c I , W R , W I ) T bb X -= (a_R, a_I, b_R, b_I, c_R, c_I, W_R, W_I)^{sf(T)} and the output quantity Y ≡ ( Γ R , Γ I ) T bb Y -= (Gamma_R, Gamma_I)^{sf(T)} using the bivariate measurement model

example.name: Reflectometer calibration
example.p: The calibration of a reflectometer () is typically undertaken by measuring values W bb W of the uncorrected reflection coefficient corresponding to a number of standards with reflection coefficients Γ bb Gamma. Often, three standards are used, giving the three (complex) simultaneous equations

for j = 1 , 2 , 3 j = 1, 2, 3. Separation of these equations into real and imaginary parts gives rise to six simultaneous linear equations that are solved for estimates of the real and imaginary parts of the calibration coefficients a bb(a), b bb(b) and c bb(c) given estimates of the real and imaginary parts of the uncorrected reflection coefficients W j bb(W)_j and of the reflection coefficients Γ j bb(Gamma)_j for the standards.

In terms of the general notation, N = 12 N = 12, m = 6 m = 6, X ≡ ( W 1 , R , W 1 , I , Γ 1 , R , Γ 1 , I , W 2 , R , W 2 , I , Γ 2 , R , Γ 2 , I , W 3 , R , W 3 , I , Γ 3 , R , Γ 3 , I ) T bb X -= (W_{1, "R"}, W_{1, "I"}, Gamma_{1, "R"}, Gamma_{1, "I"}, W_{2,"R"}, W_{2,"I"}, Gamma_{2,"R"}, Gamma_{2,"I"}, W_{3,"R"}, W_{3,"I"}, Gamma_{3,"R"}, Gamma_{3,"I"})^{sf(T)} and Y ≡ ( a R , a I , b R , b I , c R , c I ) T bb Y -= (a_R, a_I, b_R, b_I, c_R, c_I)^{sf(T)}.

The input and output quantities are related by a multivariate measurement model, where, for j = 1 , 2 , 3 j = 1, 2, 3, h 2 j − 1 ( Y , X ) h_{2j-1}(bb Y,bb X) and h 2 j ( Y , X ) h_{2j}(bb Y,bb X) are, respectively, the real and imaginary parts of the left-hand side of .

An estimate y ≡ ( a ^ R , a ^ I , b ^ R , b ^ I , c ^ R , c ^ I ) T bb y -= (hat a_R, hat a_I, hat b_R, hat b_I, hat c_R, hat c_I)^{sf(T)} of the (complex) calibration coefficients is given by using the estimates of W j bb W_j and Γ j bb Gamma_j in and solving these equations numerically.

If a computer system capable of operating with complex quantities is available, separation of these equations into real and imaginary parts is unnecessary. The equations can be solved “directly” for a bb a, b bb b and c bb c.
The jth equation involves only the four input quantities W j , R , W j , I , Γ j , R W_{j,R}, W_{j,I}, Gamma_{j,R} and Γ j , I Gamma_{j,I}.

clause.title: Propagation of uncertainty for models involving complex quantitiesGUM uncertainty frameworkexamples of law of propagation of uncertainty measurement functioncomplex measurement modelcomplex quantitycomplex
clause.p: covers the algebraically efficient determination of the partial derivatives of first order of complex multivariate measurement functions. These derivatives are needed in a particularization of the law of propagation of uncertainty to such models. The treatment can be extended to complex multivariate measurement models in general.

clause.title: Coverage region for a vector output quantitycoverage regionfrom GUM uncertainty framework

clause.p: In electrical metrology, for example, it is appropriate to treat a vector output quantity as a single entity and in dealing with summaries of the joint probability distribution for this quantity to maintain as much information as possible.

Given an estimate y bb y of the output quantity Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T bb Y = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)}, its associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) and a coverage probability p p, it is required to specify a region R Y R_{bb Y} in m-dimensional space that contains Y bb Y with probability p p.

If
y
bb y and
U y
bb(U_y) constitute the only available information about
Y
bb Y, according to the principle of maximum entropy the Gaussian PDF
N
(
y
,
U y
)
N(bb y, bb(U_y)) is used to describe the state of knowledge of
Y
bb Y [6.4.8].
This choice of PDF is consistent, within the GUM uncertainty framework, with the characterization of a single scalar output quantity Y Y by a univariate Gaussian PDF when the associated degrees of freedom is infinite.

In general, once the PDF for Y bb Y is established, either the coverage probability for a specified coverage region or a coverage region for a specified coverage probability can be determined. Doing so is straightforward for a Gaussian PDF (see , and ). For other PDFs, a numerical method such as a Monte Carlo method (see ) is helpful in obtaining approximate solutions that are acceptable for practical purposes.

The determination of a coverage region for a bivariate quantity (see ) is used to motivate the treatment for the more general multivariate case (see ). Consideration is also given to the determination of a coverage region for a quantity representing the average of a set of indication values regarded as random draws made independently from a multivariate Gaussian distribution (see ).

The problem of specifying bivariate coverage regions illustrates the aspects that distinguish the determination of multivariate coverage regions from the univariate case. Consider a point Y = ( Y 1 , Y 2 ) T bb Y = (Y_1, Y_2)^{sf(T)} in the plane with rectangular co-ordinates Y 1 Y_1 (abscissa) and Y 2 Y_2 (ordinate). Both co-ordinates of Y bb Y are measured with the same calibrated instrument. The information about Y bb Y constitutes estimates y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2 of the co-ordinates, standard uncertainties u ( y 1 ) u(y_1) and u ( y 2 ) u(y_2) associated with the estimates, as well as the covariance u ( y 1 , y 2 ) u(y_1, y_2) due to the use of the same instrument in obtaining the estimates.

In the context of the GUM uncertainty framework, as there is no other information, the joint PDF g Y 1 , Y 2 ( η 1 , η 2 ) g_{Y_1, Y_2 (eta_1, eta_2) characterizing Y bb Y is the bivariate Gaussian PDF N ( y , U y ) N(bb y, bb(U_y)) (see ) with
Two particular coverage regions are considered:

li.p: An ellipse centred at y bb y 38:
with k p k_p a constant determined such that the area under the PDF over the elliptical region is equal to p p. With this approach, the mutual dependence of Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 is taken into account.
When Y bb Y is characterized by a (bivariate) Gaussian probability distribution, the quantity
is characterized by a chi-squared distribution with two degrees of freedom. It follows that k p 2 k_p^2 is given by an upper percentage point of this chi-squared distribution, and satisfies
where X 2 2 sf(X)_2^2 has a chi-squared distribution with two degrees of freedom. For the coverage probability p = 0.95 p = 0.95, k p = 2.45 k_p = 2.45 (see ),

A rectangle centred at y bb y with sides parallel to the axes and equal to the lengths of separately determined coverage intervals for Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2. The coverage interval for Y 1 Y_1 is determined from the PDF for Y 1 Y_1 given by marginalization.

irrespective of the state of knowledge of Y 2 Y_2, and similarly for the coverage interval for Y 2 Y_2. The coverage intervals y j ± k q u ( y j ) y_j +- k_q u(y_j) are determined for coverage probability
This rectangle constitutes a coverage region for Y bb Y corresponding to a coverage probability of at least p p .

When Y bb Y is characterized by a (bivariate) Gaussian probability distribution, the marginal distribution for Y 1 Y_1 is Gaussian as is that for Y 2 Y_2. It follows that k q k_q is given by an upper percentage point of the standard Gaussian distribution, and satisfies

where Z Z has the standard Gaussian distribution N ( 0 , 1 ) N(0, 1). For the coverage probability p = 0.95 p = 0.95, the coverage intervals are y j ± k q u ( y j ) y_j +- k_q u(y_j), for j = 1 , 2 j = 1, 2, with q = 0.975 q = 0.975 and k q = 2.24 k_q = 2.24 (see ).

note.p: In the context of the GUM uncertainty framework, the elliptical coverage region given in is the smallest 100 p % 100p % coverage region.
When Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 are mutually independent, q q in can be replaced by q = p 1 / 2 q = p^{1//2}.

example.p: Consider a bivariate quantity Y bb Y characterized by the Gaussian probability distribution N ( y , U y ) N(bb y,bb(U_y)), where

(left) shows 95 % elliptical and rectangular coverage regions for Y bb Y, obtained as in and . Also shown are 1 000 points representing random draws from this probability distribution. The ellipse is the smallest coverage region for the stipulated coverage probability. 950 of the 1 000 points are contained within the elliptical coverage region, which has area 26.6 unit 2 26.6 " unit"^2, and 953 within the rectangular region, which has area 28.4 unit 2 28.4" unit"^2.
Consider a bivariate quantity Y bb Y characterized by the Gaussian probability distribution N ( y , U y ) N(bb y,bb(U_y)), where

(right) shows 95 % elliptical and rectangular coverage regions for Y bb Y, obtained as in and . Unlike example 1, the component quantities Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 of Y bb Y are (positively) correlated. 957 of the 1 000 points are contained within the elliptical coverage region, which has area 11.8 unit 2 11.8 " unit"^2, and 972 within the rectangular region, which has area 40.1 unit 2 40.1 " unit"^2, indicating that the coverage probability for the rectangular region exceeds 0.95. The rectangular region, whose shape does not reflect the correlated component quantities and the distribution of the randomly drawn points, might be considered inappropriate as a coverage region for Y bb Y. A rectangle with sides parallel to the axes of the ellipse would have smaller area and might be considered more appropriate, but might be inconvenient since it would be expressed in terms of variables that would be artificial in terms of the application.

clause.p: Further examples of coverage regions for the output quantities of bivariate measurement models are given in .

clause.title: Multivariate casechi-squared distribution coverage factor coverage interval coverage regionhyper-ellipsoidal coverage regionhyper-rectangular coverage regionmultivariate
clause.p: For more than two quantities, coverage regions are no longer easily visualized, but can be constructed in an analogous manner to those described in and for the bivariate case. A region R Y R_{bb Y} in the m-dimensional space of the output quantity Y = ( Y 1 , … , Y m ) T Y = (Y_1, ..., Y_m)^{sf(T)} is required such that the probability that Y bb Y lies in R Y R_{bb Y} is equal to the stipulated coverage probability p p. The following two forms of R Y R_{bb Y}, analogous to those described in and , can be considered.

figure.name: Elliptical and rectangular coverage regions for a bivariate quantity Y bb Y characterized by a Gaussian probability distribution for which the component quantities Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 are mutually independent, and (right) correlated ( and )

li.p: A hyper-ellipsoid (or multivariate ellipse) in m m dimensions given by .

When Y bb Y is characterized by a (multivariate) Gaussian probability distribution, the quantity is characterized by a chi-squared distribution with m degrees of freedom. It follows that k p 2 k_p^2 is given by an upper percentage point of this chi-squared distribution, and satisfies

where X m 2 sf(X)_m^2 has a chi-squared distribution with m m degrees of freedom. gives values for the coverage factor k p k_p for the coverage probability p = 0.95 p = 0.95 and m jointly evaluated Gaussian quantities for a selection of values of m m.

A hyper-rectangle (or multivariate rectangle) in m m dimensions centered at y bb y with sides equal to the lengths of separately determined coverage intervals for y j y_j, j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m. The coverage intervals are determined for coverage probability q = 1 − ( 1 − p ) / m q = 1 - (1 - p)//m. This hyper-rectangle constitutes a coverage region for Y bb Y corresponding to a coverage probability of at least p p . The coverage interval for each y j y_j is calculated following marginalization.

When Y bb Y is characterized by a (multivariate) Gaussian probability distribution, the marginal distribution for each Y j Y_j is Gaussian. It follows that the coverage factor k q k_q is given by an upper percentage point of the standard Gaussian distribution as in . gives values for k q k_q for the coverage probability p = 0.95 p = 0.95 and m m jointly evaluated Gaussian quantities for a selection of values of m m.

When the y j y_j are mutually independent, q q can be replaced by q = p 1 / m q = p^{1//m}.
