example.p: Consider n = 12 n = 12 repeated indication values in the form of pairs, representing measured values of the volume fractions of microcline ( A 1 A_1) and biotite ( A 2 A_2) in one thin section cut from G-2 granite , . shows these pairs as dots, together with a 95 % elliptical coverage region for the expectation of a quantity A bb A of dimension 2 × 1 2 xx 1. The expectation and covariance matrix for the indication values are
The 95th percentile of the F 2 , 10 F_{2,10} distribution is 4.10. A 95% coverage region for A bb A is the ellipse with equation
The small number of indication values prevents any meaningful, critical assessment of whether the assumptions hold to validate this coverage region.

clause.p: This clause gives information about the implementation of a Monte Carlo method (MCM) for the propagation of distributions: see the procedure given in , which is presented diagrammatically in .

figure.name: Twelve indication values in the form of pairs and 95% elliptical coverage region for the expectation taken as the average of these pairs ()

clause.p: MCM provides a general approach to obtain an approximate numerical representation G bb G, say, of the distribution function G Y ( η ) G_{bb Y}(bb eta) for Y bb Y 75. The heart of the approach is making repeated draws from the PDFs for the X i X_i (or joint PDF for X bb X) and the evaluation of the vector output quantity in each case.

Since G Y ( η ) G_{bb Y}(bb eta) encodes all the information known about Y bb Y, any property of Y bb Y such as expectation, variances and covariances, and coverage regions can be approximated using G bb G. The quality of these calculated results generally improves with the number of draws made.

The set of output quantity values obtained in are taken as drawn independently from the joint probability distribution for Y bb Y. Expectations, variances (and higher moments) and covariances can be determined directly from these values. The determination of coverage regions requires these values to be analyzed in an appropriate manner (see ).

Let y r bb(y_r), for r = 1 , … , M r = 1, ..., M, represent the output quantity values in . The expectation E ( Y ) bb(E(Y)) and variance V ( Y ) bb(V(Y)) of Y bb Y can be approximated using the y r bb(y_r). In general, the moments of Y bb Y (including E ( Y ) bb(E(Y)) and V ( Y ) bb(V(Y))) are approximated by the moments of these values. Let M y 0 M_{bb(y_0)} denote the number of y r bb(y_r) for which each component is no greater than the corresponding component of y 0 bb(y_0), any prescribed numerical vector of dimension m × 1 m xx 1. The probability Pr ( Y ≤ y 0 ) "Pr"(bb(Y) <= bb(y_0)) is approximated by M y 0 / M M_{bb(y_0)}//M. In this way, y 1 , … , y M bb y_1, ...,bb y_M provide a discrete representation of the distribution function G Y ( η ) G_{bb Y}(bb eta).
G bb G, the primary output from MCM, constitutes the matrix of dimension m × M m xx M given by

MCM as an implementation of the propagation of distributions when Y bb Y can be expressed explicitly in terms of X bb X, where M M is provided in advance (see otherwise), is shown diagrammatically in . MCM can be stated as a step-by-step procedure.

li.p: select the number M M of Monte Carlo trials to be made. See ,
generate M M vectors, by drawing randomly from the PDFs assigned to the input quantities X i X_i (or the joint PDF for X bb X), as realizations of the (set of N) X i X_i. See ,
for each such vector, form the corresponding value of Y bb Y, yielding M vector output quantity values in all. See ,
take the representation G bb G of the distribution function for Y bb Y as the set of M M vector output quantity values. See ,
use G bb G to form an estimate y bb y of Y bb Y and the covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) associated with y bb y. See ,
use G bb G to form an appropriate coverage region for Y bb Y, for a stipulated coverage probability p p. See .

note.p: Mathematically, the average of the M M vector output quantity values is a realization of a random variable with expectation E ( Y ) bb(E(Y)) and variance V ( Y ) / M bb(V(Y))//M. Thus, the closeness of agreement between this average and E ( Y ) bb(E(Y)) can be expected to be proportional to M − 1 / 2 M^{-1//2}.

clause.p: The effectiveness of MCM to determine y bb y, U y bb(U_y) and a coverage region for Y bb Y depends on the use of an adequately large value of M M (step ). Guidance on obtaining such a value and generally on implementing MCM is available . Also see .

clause.title: Number of Monte Carlo trialsMonte Carlo method (MCM)number of trials in

clause.p: A value of M M, the number of Monte Carlo trials, i.e. the number of vector output quantity values, needs to be selected. It can be chosen a priori, in which case there will be no direct control over the quality of the numerical results provided by MCM. The reason is that the number of trials needed to provide these results to a prescribed numerical tolerance will depend on the “shape” of the PDF for the output quantity and on the coverage probability required. Also, the calculations are stochastic in nature, being based on random draws.

Because there is no guarantee that any specific pre-assigned number will suffice, a procedure that selects
M
M adaptively, i.e. as the trials progress, can be used. Subclause provides such a procedure, a property of which is that the number of trials taken is economically consistent with the expectation of achieving a required numerical tolerance.
If the model is complicated, e.g. involving the solution of a finite-element model, because of large computing times it may not be possible to use a sufficiently large value of M M to obtain adequate distributional knowledge of the output quantity. In such a case an approximate approach would be to regard g Y ( η ) g_{bb Y}(bb eta) as Gaussian (as in the GUM) and proceed as follows. A relatively small value of M M, 50 or 100, for example, would be used. The average and covariance matrix of the resulting M M values of Y bb Y would be taken as y bb y and U y bb(U_y), respectively. Given this information, a Gaussian PDF N ( y , U y ) N(bb y,bb(U_y)) would be assigned to characterize the knowledge of Y bb Y [ 6.4.7] and a desired coverage region for Y bb Y calculated. Although this use of a small value of M M is inevitably less reliable than that of a large value in that it does not provide an approximation to the PDF for Y bb Y, it does take account of model non-linearity.

clause.title: Making draws from probability distributionsMonte Carlo method (MCM)making random draws from probability distributions used in

clause.p: In an implementation of MCM, M M vectors x r x_r, r = 1 , … , M r = 1, ..., M, are drawn from the PDFs g X i ( ξ i ) g_{X_i} (xi_i) for the input quantities X 1 , … , X N X_1, ..., X_N. Draws would be made from the joint (multivariate) PDF g X ( ξ ) g_{bb(X)}(bb(xi)) if appropriate.

Recommendations concerning the manner in which these draws can be obtained for the commonest distributions, including the rectangular, Gaussian, t-distribution and multivariate Gaussian, are given in GUM Supplement 1 [ 6.4, C]. It is possible to draw at random from any other distribution [ C.2]. Some such distributions could be approximations to distributions based on Monte Carlo results from a previous uncertainty calculation [ 6.5, D].

A procedure to make draws form a further multivariate distribution, the multivariate t-distribution, is described in .
For the results of MCM to be statistically valid, it is necessary that the pseudo-random number generators used to draw from the distributions required have appropriate properties. Some tests of randomness of the numbers produced by a generator are indicated in of GUM Supplement 1.

figure.name: Propagation and summarizing stages of uncertainty evaluation using MCM to implement the propagation of distributions when the vector output quantity can be expressed explicitly in terms of the input quantities ()

clause.title: Evaluation of the vector output quantityMonte Carlo method (MCM)evaluation of vector output quantity in

clause.p: The vector output quantity is evaluated for each of the M M draws from the PDFs for the N N input quantities. Specifically, denote the M M draws by x 1 , … , x M x_1, ..., x_M, each of dimension N × 1 N xx 1, where the rth draw x r x_r contains x 1 , r , … , x N , r x_{1,r}, ..., x_{N,r}, with x i , r x_{i,r} a draw from the PDF for X i X_i. When the measurement model is explicit the vector output quantity values are

note.p: Measurement function and derivative evaluations are made at the estimates of the input quantities when applying the law of propagation of uncertainty using exact derivatives. Measurement function evaluations only are made when applying the law of propagation of uncertainty using numerical (finite-difference) approximations to derivatives. These evaluations are made, if the GUM recommendation [ 5.1.32] is adopted, at the estimates of the input quantities and at points perturbed by u ( x i ) u(x_i) and − u ( x i ) -u(x_i) from each individual estimate x i x_i in turn. With MCM, measurement function evaluations are made in the neighbourhood of these estimates, viz. at points that can be expected to be up to several standard uncertainties away from these estimates. The fact that measurement function evaluations are made at different points according to the approach used may raise issues regarding the numerical procedure used to evaluate the function, e.g. ensuring its convergence (where iterative schemes are used) and numerical stability. The user should ensure that, where appropriate, the numerical methods used to evaluate the measurement function are valid for a sufficiently large region containing these estimates. Only occasionally would it be expected that this aspect is critical.

clause.p: The necessary modifications are made to if the X i X_i are not independent and hence a joint PDF is assigned to them.
The necessary modifications are made to if the measurement model is implicit. The vector output quantity values y r bb(y_r) are obtained as the solutions to the equations

clause.title: Discrete representation of the distribution function for the output quantitydistribution functionfrom Monte Carlo method Monte Carlo method (MCM)making random draws from probability distributions used in
clause.p:
A discrete representation of the distribution function for the vector output quantity is formed from the
M
M values of the vector output quantity obtained in . In general, this representation is a matrix
G
bb G of dimension
m × M
m xx M whose rth column is the rth value of the vector output quantity. For a univariate model,
G
bb G is a row vector.
The jth row of G bb G provides a discrete representation of an approximation to the marginal distribution for Y j Y_j.
G bb G has a graphical interpretation for m = 1 , 2 , 3 m = 1, 2, 3. Consider m = 2 m = 2 and, for r = 1 , … , M r = 1, ..., M, plot a point in the Y 1 Y_1, Y 2 Y_2 plane having co-ordinates corresponding to the two elements in column r r of G bb G. For M sufficiently large, the local density of points in any small region of the plane will be approximately proportional to the probability density there.
A variety of information can be deduced from G bb G, such as higher-order moments. However, only the expectation and variance, used as an estimate of Y bb Y and an approximation to the associated covariance matrix, respectively, are to be used as the basis for uncertainty propagation in a further stage of uncertainty evaluation [ 0.4] in the context of the GUM uncertainty framework.
If Y bb Y is to become a vector input quantity for a further uncertainty calculation, making random draws from its probability distribution is readily carried out by drawing randomly from the y r , r = 1 , … , M bb y_r, r = 1, ..., M, (or equivalently from the columns of G bb G) with equal probability [6.5].

clause.title: Estimate of the output quantity and the associated covariance matrixaverage covariance matrix covariance matrixfrom Monte Carlo method estimate of input quantitiesfrom Monte Carlo method
clause.p: The average and covariance matrix,

clause.p:
are taken, respectively, as an estimate
y
bb y of
Y
bb Y and the covariance matrix
U y
bb(U_y) associated with

bb y.
Irrespective of whether the measurement model is linear or non-linear, in the limit as M M tends to infinity, y ^ hat(bb(y)) approaches in probability E ( f ( X ) ) bb(E(f(X))) when the latter exists.

clause.title: Coverage region for a vector output quantitycoverage regionfrom Monte Carlo method
Generalcoverage regionhyper-ellipsoidal coverage regionhyper-rectangular coverage regionsmallest
clause.p: There are arbitrarily many 100 p % 100p % coverage regions for Y bb Y. Three forms of coverage regions are given here. They are considered to have practical value, but other coverage regions might sometimes be useful in particular circumstances. Each form of coverage region addressed is based on G bb G, namely, a set of M M points y r bb(y_r) randomly drawn from the PDF for Y bb Y, such as obtained with a Monte Carlo method (see ). The forms of coverage regions considered are as follows.

li.p: Hyper-ellipsoidal coverage region, which will be close to the smallest coverage region for Y bb Y when to a good approximation the PDF for Y bb Y is Gaussian,
Hyper-rectangular coverage region, which has a simple interpretation but is often pessimistically large,
Smallest coverage region, which in general does not have any particular geometric definition and is obtained to a degree of approximation that depends on M M.

clause.title: Hyper-ellipsoidal coverage regionCholesky decomposition coverage factor coverage regionhyper-ellipsoidal
clause.p: A 100 p % 100p% hyper-ellipsoidal coverage region for Y bb Y is
where y bb y specifies its location, U y bb(U_y) specifies its shape, and k p k_p specifies its size. A procedure for determining such a coverage region is as follows.

li.p: Transform the points y r bb y_r, denoting the transformed points by y . r dot(bb y)_r, according to

stem.asciimath: dot(bb y)_r = bb L^{-1} (bb y_r - bb y), " " " " r = 1, ..., M,

li.p: where L bb L is the lower triangular matrix of dimension m × m m xx m given by the Cholesky decomposition U y = L L T bb(U_y) = bb(LL)^{sf T},
Sort the transformed points y . r dot(bb y)_r according to increasing value of d r d_r, where

stem.asciimath: d_r^2 = dot(bb y)_r^{sf T} dot(bb y)_r = sum_{j=1}^{m} dot(y)_{j,r}^2, " " " " r = 1, ..., M;

li.p: Use the sorted y . r dot(bb y)_r to determine the coverage factor k p k_p such that a fraction p p of the y . r dot(bb y)_r satisfies d r < k p d_r < k_p;
Take the hyper-ellipsoid defined by as the boundary of a 100 p % 100p% coverage region for Y bb Y.

note.p: This procedure is based on reference , where multidimensional data is sorted using the metric

stem.asciimath: (bb y_r - bb a)^{sf(T)} sum^{-1}(bb y_r - bb a),

note.p: with a denoting a location statistic and ∑ sum a dispersion statistic. The choice made here is a = y bb a = bb y and ∑ = U y bb sum = bb(U_y). The procedure is also based on transforming the points so that the transformed points have a covariance matrix equal to the identity matrix, and hence there is no correlation associated with the transformed points. The points can then be sorted. The points y r bb y_r, which can be regarded as a cluster of points centered on y bb y, approximate the distribution for Y bb Y, as a consequence of the approach used to obtain them. A coverage region can be defined by the hyper-ellipsoid centred on y bb y that (just) contains 100 p % 100p% of these y r bb y_r.

The extent to which the coverage region obtained is appropriate depends on the context. It might be inappropriate if the distribution of the points y r bb(y_r) differed appreciably from a multivariate Gaussian PDF.
The matrix L bb L in step can be determined as in reference , for instance. See example.
This procedure takes into account the mutual dependence between pairs of components of Y bb Y.

For a complicated measurement model, for example, involving a finite-element calculation, because of large computing times it may not be possible to use a sufficiently large value of M M to obtain adequate distributional knowledge of the output quantity. In such a case an approximate approach would be to regard the distribution for Y bb Y as Gaussian and proceed as follows. A relatively small value of M M, say 50 or 100, would be used. The average and covariance matrix of the resulting M M values of Y bb Y would be taken as y bb y and U y bb(U_y), respectively. Given this information, a Gaussian PDF with expectation y bb y and covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) would be used to characterize the knowledge of Y bb Y, and a coverage region for Y bb Y calculated accordingly. Although this use of a small value of M M is inevitably less reliable than that of a large value in that it does not provide an approximation to the PDF for Y bb Y, it does take account of model non-linearity. gives values of k p k_p for coverage probability p = 0.95 p = 0.95 as a function of m m, the number of output quantities, under the assumption that Y bb Y is Gaussian.

example.p: Consider the measurement model

where the input quantities X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 are each assigned the Gaussian distribution N ( 0 , 0.1 ) N(0, 0.1), X 3 X_3 is assigned the rectangular distribution R ( − ( 3 × 1.9 ) 1 / 2 R(-(3 xx 1.9)^{1//2}, ( 3 × 1.9 ) 1 / 2 ) (3 xx 1.9)^{1//2}), and the quantities are (pairwise) independent. The expectations of the input quantities X i X_i are x i = 0 x_i = 0, i = 1 , 2 , 3 i = 1, 2, 3, with associated variances u 2 ( ξ ) = 0.1 u^2(xi) = 0.1, i = 1 , 2 i = 1, 2, and u 2 ( x 3 ) = 1.9 u^2(x_3) = 1.9. Then, as in , Y = ( Y 1 , Y 2 ) T bb Y = (Y_1, Y_2)^{sf(T)} has expectation and covariance matrix
The y . r dot(bb y)_r in step are formed using L − 1 bb L^{-1}, where to three decimal places,

(left) shows 1 000 points randomly drawn from the probability distribution for Y bb Y defined by the measurement model and the above Gaussian probability distributions for the input quantities X i X_i, i = 1 , 2 , 3 i = 1, 2, 3. The 95% elliptical coverage region for Y bb Y based on characterizing Y bb Y by the bivariate Gaussian distribution N ( y , U y ) N(bb y,bb(U_y)) (as in ) is shown by a broken line. This region has area 11.8 unit 2 11.8 " unit"^2, k p = 2.45 k_p = 2.45 and contains 968 of the 1 000 points. The elliptical coverage region determined on the basis of the 1 000 draws (as in the above procedure) is shown by a solid line. This region has area 10.6 unit 2 10.6 " unit"^2, k p = 2.33 k_p = 2.33, that is, it is slightly smaller than the region obtained under the Gaussian assumption, and, by construction, contains exactly 950 points.
The measurement model is considered in more detail in , and further examples of coverage regions for the output quantities in bivariate measurement models are also given in .

clause.title: Hyper-rectangular coverage regioncoverage regionhyper-rectangular
clause.p: A 100 p % 100p% hyper-rectangular coverage region for Y bb Y is

where y = ( y 1 , … , y m ) T bb y = (y_1, ..., y_m)^{sf(T)} specifies its location, u ( y ) = ( u ( y 1 ) , … , u ( y m ) ) T bb(u(y)) = (u(y_1), ..., u(y_m))^{sf(T)} specifies its shape, and k q k_q specifies its size. A procedure for determining such a coverage region is as follows:

li.p: Transform the points y r bb(y_r) according to , denoting the transformed points by y . r dot(bb y)_r, where L bb L is now the diagonal matrix of dimension m × m m xx m with diagonal elements u ( y 1 ) , … , u ( y m ) u(y_1), ..., u(y_m),
Rank the transformed points y r bb y_r according to increasing value of d r d_r, where now
Use the ranked y . r dot(bb y)_r to determine the coverage factor k q k_q such that a fraction p p of the y . r dot(bb y)_r satisfies d r < k q d_r < k_q;
Take the hyper-rectangle defined by as the boundary of a 100 p % 100p% coverage region for Y bb Y.

note.p: With this approach, mutual dependence between pairs of components of Y bb Y is not taken into account.

example.p: (right) shows the same 1 000 points as in (left). The 95% rectangular coverage region for Y bb Y based on characterizing Y j Y_j, j = 1 , 2 j = 1, 2, by the Gaussian distribution N ( y j , u 2 ( y j ) ) N(y_j, u^2(y_j)), and ignoring the correlation associated with y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2 (as in ), is shown by a broken line. This region has area 40.1 unit 2 40.1 " unit"^2, k q = 2.24 k_q = 2.24 and contains all 1 000 points. The rectangular coverage region determined on the basis of the 1 000 draws (as in the above procedure) is shown by a solid line. This region has area 25.5 unit 2 25.5 " unit"^2, k q = 1.78 k_q = 1.78, that is, it is smaller than the region obtained under the Gaussian assumption, and, by construction, contains exactly 950 points.

clause.title: Smallest coverage regioncoverage regionsmallest
clause.p: A procedure that provides an approximation to the smallest 100 p % 100p% coverage region is as follows.

li.p: Construct a (hyper-)rectangular region in the space of the output quantities;
Subdivide this rectangular region into a mesh of small rectangles;
Assign each output quantity value to the small rectangle containing it;
Use the fraction of output quantity values assigned to each rectangle as the approximate probability that Y bb Y lies in that rectangle;
List the rectangles in terms of decreasing probability;
Form the cumulative sum of probabilities for these listed rectangles, stopping when this sum is no smaller than p p;
Take the corresponding set of rectangles as defining the smallest coverage region.

note.p: The procedure is based on reference and consists of subdividing the space of the output quantities into a number of small (hyper-)rectangles, approximating the probability that Y bb Y lies in each small rectangle by the proportion of output quantity values assigned to that rectangle, and approximating the smallest coverage region by the smallest set of such rectangles that contain 100 p % 100p% of the M M output quantity values.
The rectangular region in step should just contain all the y r bb y_r.
The fineness of the mesh in influences the approximate coverage region produced.

The quality of the approximation generally improves with M M. To achieve a sufficiently good approximation, particularly for a number of output quantities larger than about two or three, might necessitate a very large value of M M.
The region so obtained might be disjointed, particularly if M M is insufficiently large.

Step sets the probability for each rectangle to be the relative frequency of output quantity values assigned to that rectangle. A coverage region that is less disjointed and having a smoother boundary would be expected to be obtained if step were replaced by the use of a more sophisticated approximation to the probability density .

Some of the approximate probabilities in step may be equal in value. In such a case the order of the items in the list in step is not unique. As a consequence a different approximate coverage region might be obtained for each possible ordering.
In the bivariate case ( m = 2 m = 2), steps to are also carried out in the initial stages of a typical contouring algorithm used in visualizing an approximation to the PDF for Y bb Y (see ).

example.p: Consider the same problem as in . shows approximations to the 95% smallest coverage region, obtained using the procedure in this subclause, based on a set of small rectangles forming a 10 × 10 10 xx 10 mesh and (right) a 100 × 100 100 xx 100 mesh. The coverage region in (left) relates to 1 000 points drawn randomly from the PDF for Y bb Y, has area 11.3 unit 2 11.3 " unit"^2 and contains 955 of the points. The coverage region in (right) relates to 1 000 000 points drawn randomly from the PDF for Y bb Y, has area 9.4 unit 2 9.4 " unit"^2 and contains 950 074 of the points. For comparison, in each figure, the 95% elliptical coverage region for Y bb Y based on the procedure in is shown by a solid line.

figure.name: Elliptical coverage regions based on and approximations to the smallest coverage region based on for a 10 × 10 10 xx 10 mesh and 1 000 points drawn randomly from the PDF for Y bb Y, and (right) for a 100 × 100 100 xx 100 mesh and 1 000 000 points ()

clause.title: Adaptive Monte Carlo procedureMonte Carlo method (MCM)adaptive procedure for

clause.p: The effectiveness of MCM to determine estimates y bb y of the output quantities Y bb Y, the associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) and a coverage region for Y bb Y depends on the use of an adequately large value for the number M M of Monte Carlo trials. A value for M M can be chosen a priori as in . Alternatively, an adaptive Monte Carlo procedure can be used that involves carrying out an increasing number of Monte Carlo trials until the various results of interest have stabilized in a statistical sense. A numerical result is deemed to have stabilized if twice the standard deviation associated with it is less than a stipulated numerical tolerance (see ).
The objective of the adaptive procedure given in is to provide

li.p: an estimate y = ( y 1 , … , y m ) T bb y = (y_1, ..., y_m)^{sf(T)} of Y bb Y,
a vector u ( y ) = ( u ( y 1 ) , … , u ( y m ) ) T bb(u(y)) = (u(y_1), ..., u(y_m))^{sf(T)} of standard uncertainties associated with the estimates,
a positive definite matrix R Y R_{bb Y} of dimension m × m m xx m of correlation coefficients r i j = r ( y i , y j ) r_{ij} = r(y_i, y_j) associated with pairs of the estimates, and

a coverage factor k p k_p defining a 100 p % 100p% coverage region for Y bb Y in the form of a hyper-ellipsoid in m dimensions, such that each of these values can be expected to meet a stipulated numerical tolerance.

note.p: By its stochastic nature, the procedure cannot be guaranteed to meet these tolerances.
Generally, the larger is the coverage probability p p, the larger is the number of Monte Carlo trials required to determine k p k_p for a given numerical tolerance.
The standard uncertainties u ( y ) bb(u(y)) and correlation matrix R Y R_{bb Y} together determine the covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) associated with the estimates y bb y (see and ).

When a coverage region is to be determined that is not in the form of a hyper-ellipsoid in m m dimensions, a test for stabilization can be made in terms of the parameters defining the region. For example, for a coverage region in the form of a hyper-rectangle in m dimensions, a test could be made in terms of the coverage factor k q k_q defining the region and a numerical tolerance associated with k q k_q. The procedure given in is modified accordingly.

When a coverage region is not required, an increasing number of Monte Carlo trials is undertaken until the estimates y bb y, associated standard uncertainties u ( y ) bb(u(y)) and correlation matrix R Y R_{bb Y} have stabilized.
When dependencies exist among the output quantities, R Y R_{bb Y} may not be positive definite. See (particularly ) for an example.

clause.title: Numerical tolerance associated with a numerical valuenumerical tolerance

li.p: express z z in the form c × 10 e ≪ c xx 10^{ell}, where c c is an n dig n_{"dig"} decimal digit integer and e ≪ ell an integer;
take

clause.p: For j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m, the numerical tolerance δ j delta_j used to test for stabilization of the estimate y j y_j of Y j Y_j and the standard uncertainty u ( y j ) u(y_j) associated with y j y_j in the adaptive procedure of is calculated in terms of a number of significant decimal digits regarded as meaningful in the value of u ( y j ) u(y_j).

The numerical tolerance rho used to test for stabilization of the matrix R y bb(R_y) of correlation coefficients r i , j r_{i,j} associated with the estimate y y is calculated in terms of a number of significant decimal digits in λ max lambda_{"max"}, the largest eigenvalue of R y bb(R_y) (see ).
The matrix R y bb(R_y) would often be used in a subsequent uncertainty evaluation. Commonly, this evaluation would relate to a scalar quantity Q Q expressed as some linear combination

stem.asciimath: Q = c_1 Y_1 + cdots + c_m Y_m = bb(c)^{sf(T)} bb(Y)

clause.p: of the Y i Y_i. Using

of Q Q is given by
where

The numerical accuracy of u ( q ) u(q) depends on that of R y bb(R_y) and d d, the latter depending on D y bb(D_y) and hence u ( y ) = ( u 1 ( y 1 ) , … , u m ( y m ) ) T bb(u(y)) = (u_1(y_1), ..., u_m(y_m))^{sf(T)}.

For subsequent uncertainty evaluations that are more complicated, such as involving a least squares calculation using explicitly or implicitly the inverse of U y bb(U_y), it would be necessary to utilize a different stabilization test. For a least squares calculation, it would be appropriate to calculate rho in in terms of a number of significant decimal digits in the smallest eigenvalue of R y bb(R_y) (also see ): the sensitivity of the solution to the least squares problem depends on the condition number λ max / λ min lambda_{"max"}//lambda_{"min"} of R y bb(R_y). Hence, in order to reduce the number of Monte Carlo trials, a parametrization of that problem should be used that makes the condition number as small as reasonably possible.

The numerical tolerance κ p kappa_p used to test for stabilization of the coverage factor k p k_p is calculated in terms of a number of significant decimal digits regarded as meaningful in the value k p k_p.

For a subsequent uncertainty evaluation based on the use of G bb G (see ) as an approximation to the distribution function for Y bb Y, it should be ensured that this discrete representation is adequate for the purpose, especially when a coverage region is to be provided. Detailed advice on this aspect is beyond the scope of this Supplement.

clause.title: Adaptive procedurerandom variable
clause.p: A suggested practical approach, involving carrying out a sequence of applications of MCM, is as follows.

li.p: set n dig n_{"dig"} to an appropriate small positive integer (see ),
set M = max ( J , 10 4 ) M = max(J, 10^4), where J J is the smallest integer greater than or equal to 100 / ( 1 − p ) 100//(1 - p),
set h = 1 h = 1, denoting the first application of MCM in the sequence,
carry out M M Monte Carlo trials, as in and ,

use the M M vector output quantity values y 1 , … , y M bb y_1, ...,bb y_M so obtained to calculate y ( h ) y^{(h)}, u ( y ( h ) ) bb(u)(bb(y)^{(h)}), R y ( h ) bb(R_y)^{(h)} and k p ( h ) k_p^{(h)} as an estimate of Y bb Y, the associated standard uncertainties, the associated correlation matrix and a coverage factor for a 100 p % 100p% coverage region, respectively, i.e. for the hth member of the sequence,
if h ≤ 10 h <= 10, increase h by one and return to step ,
for j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m, calculate the standard deviation s y j s_{y_j} associated with the average of the estimates y j ( 1 ) , … , y j h y_j^{(1)}, ..., y_j^{h}, given by
calculate the counterpart of this statistic for the components of u ( y ( h ) ) bb(u)(bb(y)^{(h)}) and for λ max lambda_{"max"} and k p ( h ) k_p^{(h)};
use all h × M h xx M model values available so far to form values for u ( y ) bb(u(y)), R y bb(R_y) and k p k_p;
for j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m, calculate the numerical tolerances δ j delta_j associated with u ( y j ) u(y_j) as in and ,
calculate the numerical tolerance ρ rho associated with the matrix R y bb(R_y) of correlation coefficients as in and ,
calculate the numerical tolerance κ p kappa_p associated with k p k_p as in and ,

if for any j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m, 2 s y j 2s_{y_j} or 2 s u ( y j ) 2s_{u(y_j)} exceeds δ j delta_j, or 2 s λ max 2slambda_{"max"} exceeds ρ rho, or 2 s k p 2sk_p exceeds κ p kappa_p, increase h h by one and return to step ,

regard the overall computation as having stabilized, and use all h × M h xx M vector output quantity values obtained to calculate y bb y, U y bb(U_y) and the coverage factor k p k_p for a 100 p % 100p% coverage region, as in and .

note.p: For (stochastic) convergence of the adaptive procedure, the expectation and covariance matrix of Y bb Y must exist.
The choice of M M in step is arbitrary, but has been found suitable in practice.

The initial use of ten applications of MCM with M M trials in steps to helps to prevent premature convergence of the procedure, and, moreover, makes the assumption, implied by note 6, more reasonable. A similar change to the counterpart of this procedure when there is a single scalar output quantity [ 7.9.4] would also improve the performance of the scheme there for some problems.
In step , y j y_j is regarded as a realization of a random variable with standard deviation s y j s_{y_j}.
The standard deviations formed in steps and tend to reduce in a manner proportional to h − 1 / 2 h^{-1//2} (see ).
The factor 2 used in step is based on regarding the averages as realizations of Gaussian variables, and corresponds to a coverage probability of approximately 95 %.
Reference considers improvements to the adaptive scheme of GUM Supplement 1 [ 7.9].

clause.title: Validation of the GUM uncertainty framework using a Monte Carlo methodGUM uncertainty frameworkvalidation of

clause.p: The (generalized) GUM uncertainty framework (GUF) can be expected to work well in many circumstances. However, it is not always straightforward to determine whether all the conditions for its application hold [ 5.7, 5.8]. Indeed, the degree of difficulty of doing so would typically be considerably greater than that required to apply MCM, assuming suitable software were available . Therefore, since these circumstances cannot readily be tested, any cases of doubt should be validated. Since the domain of validity for MCM is broader than that for the GUM uncertainty framework, it is recommended that both the GUM uncertainty framework and MCM be applied and the results compared. Should the comparison be favourable, the GUM uncertainty framework could be used on this occasion and for sufficiently similar problems in the future. Otherwise, consideration should be given to using MCM or another appropriate approach instead.
Specifically, it is recommended that steps and below and the comparison process in be carried out.

li.p: apply the GUM uncertainty framework (see ) to yield (i) an estimate y GUF bb(y)^{"GUF"} of Y bb Y, (ii) the standard uncertainties u ( y GUF u(bb(y)^{"GUF"}) associated with y GUF bb(y)^{"GUF"}, (iii) the correlation matrix R y GUF bb(R_y)^{"GUF"} associated with y GUF bb(y)^{"GUF"}, and (iv) the coverage factor k p GUF k_p^{"GUF"} defining a 100 p % 100p% coverage region for Y bb Y in the form of a hyper-ellipsoid in m m dimensions,
apply the adaptive Monte Carlo procedure (see ) to provide, similarly, y MCM bb y_{"MCM"}, u ( y MCM ) bb(u)(bb(y)_{"MCM"}), R y MCM bb(R_y)^{"MCM"} and k p MCM k_p^{"MCM"}.

clause.p: The objective of the comparison procedure is to determine whether the results provided by the GUM uncertainty framework and MCM agree to within a stipulated numerical tolerance. The procedure is as follows.

li.p: set n dig n_{"dig"} to an appropriate small positive integer (see ),
for j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m, calculate the numerical tolerances δ j delta_j associated with u ( y j ) u(y_j) as in and ,
calculate the numerical tolerance rho associated with the matrix R y bb(R_y) of correlation coefficients as in and ,
calculate the numerical tolerance κ p kappa_p associated with k p k_p as in and ,

compare the estimates, associated standard uncertainties and correlation coefficients, and coverage factors obtained using the GUM uncertainty framework and MCM to determine whether the required number of correct decimal digits in the results provided by the GUM uncertainty framework has been obtained. Specifically, determine

viz. the absolute differences of the respective numerical results. Then, if for all j = 1 , … , m j = 1, ..., m, d y j d_{y_j} and d u ( y j ) d_{u(y_j)} are no larger than δ j delta_j, d λ max d_{lambda_{"max"}} is no larger than ρ rho, and d k p d_{k_p} is no larger than κ p kappa_p, the comparison is favourable and the GUM uncertainty framework has been validated in this instance.

note.p: The choice of 100 p % 100p% coverage region will influence the comparison. Therefore, the validation applies for the specified coverage probability p p and coverage region only.

In situations where a coverage region is not required, the test in step can be based instead on d y j d_{y_j}, d u ( y j ) d_{u(y_j)} and d λ max d_{lambda_{"max"}} only. In cases where a coverage region is determined that is not in the form of a hyper-ellipsoid in m m dimensions, a test can be made in terms of the parameters defining the region. For example, for a coverage region in the form of a hyper-rectangle in m m dimensions, a test can be made in terms of the coverage factor k q k_q defining the region and a numerical tolerance κ q kappa_q associated with k q k_q.

A sufficient number M M of Monte Carlo trials should be performed in obtaining MCM results for the purpose of validating those from the GUM uncertainty framework. It is recommended that the numerical tolerances set in the adaptive procedure of are no greater than one fifth of the respective numerical tolerances used in the validation procedure [ 8.2]. Alternatively, the number of significant decimal digits set in the adaptive procedure can be set to be one greater than the number of digits set in the validation procedure.

clause.title: ExamplesGUM uncertainty frameworkexamples of Monte Carlo method (MCM)examples of
Illustrations of aspects of this Supplement

clause.p: The first example (see ) is a linear measurement model in which the output quantities of the measurement model depend on a common effect and on effects that are different for the output quantities. This is an example for which analytical solutions are available for some special cases.

The second example (see ) is a non-linear measurement model concerning the transformation from a Cartesian representation (in terms of real and imaginary parts) of a complex quantity to a polar representation (in terms of magnitude and phase) of the quantity. This is an example for which analytical solutions are available for some special cases .

The third example (see ), constituting a further non-linear measurement model, is that in the GUM concerned with the simultaneous measurement of resistance and reactance [ H.2]. The example illustrates the treatment of a series of simultaneous indication values of a vector quantity that have been obtained independently.

The fourth example (see ) is concerned with the measurement of Celsius temperature using a resistance thermometer. The example illustrates the treatment of a univariate measurement model and a multivariate measurement model.

Many of the figures used to illustrate the results for the examples are best viewed in colour. In the figures showing contours, contours with a common colour correspond to the same contour value. The correspondence between contours of the same colour for contour plots within the same figure is applied throughout this Supplement unless a statement is made to the contrary. When two or more figures are used to compare results, such as those from the GUM uncertainty framework and a Monte Carlo method, the same axes are generally used. There are some exceptions when the PDFs, etc. for the methods are very different.

Since the primary output from MCM is the set of M M values of the vector quantity Y bb Y gathered in the matrix G of dimension m × M m xx M (see ), it is often desirable to summarize them by an approximation to the corresponding probability density function, and to depict this function by its contour lines. The figures in this clause show such contour lines in cases where the output quantity is of dimension m = 2 m = 2. The contour lines should be drawn in ways that guarantee that, as the number M of draws from the probability distribution of the output quantity grows large, they converge to the contours of the corresponding probability density function: doing so requires appropriate smoothing , . Some contour diagrams shown in this clause are computed directly from an approximation to the corresponding probability density function. For others an appropriate smoothing algorithm is applied and the resulting smoothed contours drawn. In one instance ( ) unsmoothed and smoothed contours are shown to indicate the effect of the smoothing algorithm.

clause.title: Formulation
clause.p: This example (compare ) considers the additive (linear) bivariate measurement model

for three different sets of PDFs g X i ( ξ i ) g_{X_i}(xi_i) assigned to the input quantities X i X_i, regarded as independent. The measurement model has three input quantities X 1 X_1, X 2 X_2 and X 3 X_3, with X 3 X_3 representing an effect that is common to the output quantities Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2, and X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 effects that are different for Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2. For the first set of input PDFs (see ), each g X i ( ξ i ) g_{X_i}(xi_i) is chosen to be a standard Gaussian distribution with X i X_i having expectation zero and standard deviation unity. The second set of PDFs (see ) is identical to the first, but g X 3 ( ξ 3 ) g_{X_3}(xi_3) is a rectangular distribution, also with X 3 X_3 having expectation zero and standard deviation unity. The third set of PDFs (see ) is identical to the second except that X 3 X_3 has standard deviation three, representing a dominant common effect.

Characterize each X i X_i by a standard Gaussian distribution. The estimates of the X i X_i are x i = 0 x_i = 0, i = 1 , 2 , 3 i = 1, 2, 3, with associated standard uncertainties u ( x i ) = 1 u(x_i) = 1. The results obtained from the application of the GUM uncertainty framework (see ) and MCM (see ) are summarized in and . Some results in the table are reported to four significant decimal digits in order to facilitate their comparison.

table.name: Application of the GUM uncertainty framework (GUF) and MCM to the additive model , with each X i X_i characterized by a standard Gaussian distribution ()

clause.p: The (generalized) GUM uncertainty framework gives the estimate y = ( 0 , 0 ) T bb y = (0, 0)^{sf(T)} of Y bb Y. The covariance matrix associated with this estimate is
obtained by applying , namely U y = C x U x C x T bb(U_y) = bb(C_x) bb(U_x)C_x^{sf(T)}, with

The correlation coefficient associated with the estimates y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2 (see ) is r ( y 1 , y 2 ) = 0.5 r(y_1, y_2) = 0.5. 95% coverage regions for Y bb Y taking the forms of an ellipse and a rectangle are defined, respectively, by coverage factors k p = 2.45 k_p = 2.45 () and k q = 2.24 k_q = 2.24 (). These results are summarized in row 1 (GUF) of . In the context of the GUM uncertainty framework, given the information available, Y bb Y is characterized by the bivariate Gaussian PDF N ( y , U y ) N(bb(y),bb(U_y)).

The application of MCM with, respectively, M = 10 5 , 10 6 M = 10^5, 10^6 and 10 7 10^7 trials gives the results summarized in rows 2 to 4 (MCM) of . The fourth and fifth numerical values of M M ( 0.35 × 10 6 0.35 xx 10^6 and 0.45 × 10 6 0.45 xx 10^6) given in rows 5 and 6 (Adaptive MCM) of the table are the numbers of trials taken by two applications of the adaptive Monte Carlo procedure (see ) with n dig n_{"dig"}, the number of significant decimal digits regarded as meaningful in the numerical value of a result, set equal to three (see ). For the applications of the adaptive procedure, it is required that all the results, i.e. y 1 , y 2 , u ( y 1 ) , u ( y 2 ) , r ( y 1 , y 2 ) , k p y_1, y_2, u(y_1), u(y_2), r(y_1, y_2), k_p and k q k_q, have stabilized in a statistical sense.
The PDF for Y bb Y obtained analytically is the bivariate Gaussian PDF N ( y , U y ) N(bb(y),bb(U_y)) with y and U y bb(U_y) given in .

It also shows the approximate PDF provided by MCM with M = 10 7 M = 10^7 trials. The approximation is displayed as a scaled frequency distribution (histogram) of the model values for Y 1 Y_1 from the Monte Carlo calculation. These two PDFs are almost indistinguishable by eye. Comparable results would be obtained for Y 2 Y_2.

0.45 xx 10^6 trials. The contours are considerably less “smooth” than those in (right) obtained from a much larger number of trials. (right) provides an instance of contour smoothing (see ).
A large value for the number M M of Monte Carlo trials, and a fine discretization of the domain of Y bb Y, are generally needed to ensure that the contours in a graphical representation of the approximate PDF for Y bb Y obtained using MCM are smooth. A similar effect arises for a single scalar output quantity, where a large value for M and many classes (or bins), are generally needed to ensure that the approximate PDF displayed as a histogram (or scaled frequency distribution) is smooth.

The validation procedure of is applied to validate the results obtained from the GUM uncertainty framework against those from the two applications of the adaptive Monte Carlo calculation. The procedure is applied with n dig n_{"dig"}, the number of significant decimal digits regarded as meaningful in the numerical value of a result, set equal to two. In this case, the numerical tolerances associated with the results are, respectively,

The results obtained from the GUM uncertainty framework were validated in both instances. Excepting k q k_q, agreement between the results would be expected because all the conditions hold for the application of the GUM uncertainty framework. The evaluation of k q k_q in the GUM uncertainty framework does not take account of the mutual dependence between Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 (see ). Nevertheless, the values for k q k_q obtained agree to the required number of significant decimal digits.

As , but showing the marginal PDF for Y 1 Y_1 ()

clause.p: This case is identical to that in except that X 3 X_3 is characterized by a rectangular distribution so that X 3 X_3 has an expectation of zero and a standard deviation of unity. Again, the estimates of the X i X_i are x i = 0 x_i = 0, i = 1 , 2 , 3 i = 1, 2, 3, with associated standard uncertainties u ( x i ) = 1 u(x_i) = 1. The results from the application of the GUM uncertainty framework (see ) and MCM (see ) are summarized in and .

The GUM uncertainty framework provides exactly the same (bivariate Gaussian) PDF for Y bb Y (, left) when the PDF for X 3 X_3 is Gaussian (as in ) or rectangular (as here), because the expectations of the input quantities are identical, as are the standard deviations, in the two cases. Because the measurement model depends linearly on the input quantities, there is no approximation involved in the application of the law of propagation of uncertainty to obtain an estimate y bb y of Y bb Y and the associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y). However, the PDF provided by the GUM uncertainty framework is not the same as the analytical solution, since the latter depends on the distribution used to characterize X 3 X_3, and not only the expectation and standard deviation of the quantity.

The validation procedure of is again applied (see and ). In this case, values for y 1 , y 2 , u ( y 1 ) , u ( y 2 ) y_1, y_2, u(y_1), u(y_2) and r ( y 1 , y 2 ) r(y_1, y_2) obtained from the GUM uncertainty framework were validated against those obtained from the two applications of the adaptive Monte Carlo procedure, but the values for k p k_p and k q k_q were not validated. The value of k p k_p obtained from the GUM uncertainty framework is about 7% bigger than that obtained from MCM, and the value of k q k_q about 20% bigger.

constitute a bivariate measurement model for R R and θ theta. Since R R is a magnitude, and is known to be non-negative, it is determined uniquely by the positive square root of R 2 R^2. By using a “four quadrant inverse tangent function” (often denoted by “atan2”) θ theta is determined uniquely to satisfy − π < θ ≤ π -pi < theta <= pi. Thus can be cast as a bivariate measurement model.

index.secondary: assigning a probability density function (PDF) to

clause.p: Given are estimates x 1 x_1 and x 2 x_2 of the quantities X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2, obtained from a suitable measuring system, and associated standard uncertainties u ( x 1 ) u(x_1) and u ( x 2 ) u(x_2) and covariance u ( x 1 , x 2 ) = r u ( x 1 ) u ( x 2 ) u(x_1, x_2) = ru(x_1)u(x_2), where r = r ( x 1 , x 2 ) r = r(x_1, x_2) denotes the associated correlation coefficient [ 5.2.2]. On the basis of this information [ 6.4.8.1], X X is assigned a bivariate Gaussian PDF, with expectation and covariance matrix
The quantities X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 are assumed to have dimension one.

The determination of an estimate y bb y of Y bb Y and the associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) is considered for different choices of x 1 , x 2 , u ( x 1 ) , u ( x 2 ) x_1, x_2, u(x_1), u(x_2) and r ( x 1 , x 2 ) r(x_1, x_2).

Six cases are considered, in all of which x 2 x_2 is taken as zero and u ( x 1 ) = u ( x 2 ) = u x = 0.010 u(x_1) = u(x_2) = u_x = 0.010. The first three of these cases correspond to taking x 1 = 0.001 , 0.010 x_1 = 0.001, 0.010 and 0.100, each with r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 r(x_1, x_2) = 0 (see ). The second three cases correspond to taking the same x 1 x_1, but with r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0.9 r(x_1, x_2) = 0.9 (see ). shows the contours of the joint PDFs assigned to the input quantities X bb X for case 1 ( x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001 and r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 r(x_1, x_2) = 0) and case 4 ( x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001 and r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0.9 r(x_1, x_2) = 0.9). (In this figure a common set of contour values is not used for the two contour plots.) The contours of the joint PDFs for the other cases are obtained by translating the contours shown along the X 1 X_1-axis so that they are centred on x 1 = 0.010 x_1 = 0.010 (cases 2 and 5) or x 1 = 0.100 x_1 = 0.100 (cases 3 and 6).

clause.p: The evaluation of uncertainty is treated by applying the propagation of distributions (a) analytically (for purposes of comparison), (b) using the (generalized) GUM uncertainty framework, and © using MCM.

The (joint) PDF for Y bb Y, and the marginal PDFs for Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2, can be obtained analytically in the case that X 1 X_1 is described by the Gaussian distribution N ( x 1 , u x 2 ) N(x_1, u_x^2) and X 2 X_2 by N ( x 2 , u x 2 ) N(x_2, u_x^2), with X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 mutually independent. with associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) obtained by applying a generalization of the law of propagation of uncertainty. See and .

The analytical results for y 1 y_1 and u ( y 1 ) u(y_1) given in are determined by calculating numerically, to a high degree of approximation, E ( Y 1 ) E(Y_1) and V ( Y 1 ) V(Y_1), which take the form of definite integrals involving the marginal distribution for Y 1 Y_1 obtained analytically (see ) in ). Similarly, the analytical results for y 2 y_2 and u ( y 2 ) u(y_2) are determined by calculating numerically E ( Y 2 ) E(Y_2) and V ( Y 2 ) V(Y_2), which involve the marginal distribution for Y 2 Y_2 obtained analytically (see expression in ). It is straightforward to show that Cov ( Y 1 , Y 2 ) = 0 "Cov"(Y_1, Y_2) = 0 and, therefore, the analytical result r ( y 1 , y 2 ) = 0 r(y_1, y_2) = 0.

table.name: Co-ordinate system transformation results, for input estimates with associated zero covariance (, and )

clause.p: shows in the top three graphs the contours of the joint PDFs for Y bb Y obtained analytically, using the GUM uncertainty framework and using MCM. The PDF provided by MCM is consistent with the analytic solution. However, the PDF provided by the GUM uncertainty framework is very different from the analytic solution, and it is necessary to use a different set of contour values to illustrate this PDF (see ). Furthermore, the PDF provided by the GUM uncertainty framework takes positive values for infeasible values of the output quantities, i.e. for values η 1 < 0 eta_1 < 0, η 2 ≤ − π eta_2 <= -pi and η 2 > π eta_2 > pi, erroneously implying a non-zero probability density for such infeasible values.

shows in the bottom two graphs the marginal PDFs for the output quantities Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 obtained from the joint distribution for Y bb Y. The marginal PDFs provided by MCM (shown as histograms or scaled frequency distributions) are consistent with the analytic solutions (shown as dashed lines). However, the marginal distributions provided by the GUM uncertainty framework (shown as solid lines) are very different from the analytic solutions. For convenience of presentation, the graph of the marginal PDF for Y 2 Y_2 provided by the GUM uncertainty interval is only shown for (feasible) values of η 2 eta_2 satisfying − π < η 2 ≤ π -pi < eta_2 <= pi, although the PDF, which is the Gaussian N ( 0 , 10 2 ) N(0, 10^2), extends well beyond this interval. The marginal PDFs provided by the GUM uncertainty framework imply a non-zero probability density for infeasible values of the output quantities (see also ).

For the input estimate x 1 = 0.010 x_1 = 0.010, with correlation coefficient r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 r(x_1, x_2) = 0, rows 4 to 6 of give the results obtained analytically, using the GUM uncertainty framework (GUF) and MCM. shows the contours of the joint PDFs for Y bb Y obtained using the three approaches and the marginal PDFs for Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 obtained from the respective joint PDFs.

It is seen that the results obtained using MCM are consistent with the analytic solution. However, the results obtained using the GUM uncertainty framework are different from the analytic solution, but not so different as for the case x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001. The relative differences between the standard uncertainties u ( y 1 ) u(y_1) and u ( y 2 ) u(y_2) determined using the GUM uncertainty framework and analytically are approximately 25% and 10% (compared with approximately 40% and 470% for the case x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001).
For the input estimate x 1 = 0.100 x_1 = 0.100, with correlation coefficient r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 r(x_1, x_2) = 0, the results obtained using the three approaches are given in rows 7 to 9 of and .

It is seen that the results obtained using the GUM uncertainty framework and MCM are both consistent with the analytic solution. The marginal distributions provided by the three approaches shown in are virtually indistinguishable. The estimates, associated standard uncertainties and associated correlation coefficient determined using the three approaches agree to two significant decimal digits.

The evaluation of uncertainty is treated by applying the propagation of distributions (a) using the (generalized) GUM uncertainty framework (see ), and (b) using MCM with M = 10 7 M = 10^7 trials (see ).

For the input estimates x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001, x 1 = 0.010 x_1 = 0.010 and x 1 = 0.100 x_1 = 0.100, with correlation coefficient r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0.9 r(x_1, x_2) = 0.9, gives the results obtained. The joint PDFs for Y bb Y and the marginal PDFs for Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 obtained from the two approaches are shown for the three cases in, respectively, .

table.name: Co-ordinate system transformation results, for input estimates with associated covariance r ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0.9 r(x_1, x_2) = 0.9 ()

clause.p: For the cases x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001 and x 1 = 0.010 x_1 = 0.010 the results provided by the GUM uncertainty framework and MCM are very different. In particular, MCM gives marginal PDFs for Y 2 Y_2 in these two cases that have two peaks, and which are very different from the Gaussian (unimodal) distributions provided by the GUM uncertainty framework. The positions of these peaks in the marginal PDFs are (approximately) at the values η 2 = π / 4 ≈ 0.785 eta_2 = pi//4 ~~ 0.785 and η 2 = π / 4 − π ≈ − 2.356 eta_2 = pi//4 - pi ~~ -2.356. These values are the polar angles that define the orientation of the major axis of the elliptical contours of the joint PDF for the input quantities X bb X (see , right).

For the input estimates x 1 = 0.001 x_1 = 0.001 and x 1 = 0.010 x_1 = 0.010, and generally for values of x 1 x_1 close to zero, the effect of the non-zero covariance is to change noticeably the results provided by MCM.

clause.p:
The (numerical) results given in provide summaries of the (joint and marginal) PDFs shown in the figures. In some circumstances such summaries may not be appropriate or may be insufficient to describe the nature of the PDF characterizing the output quantity. Consider the results for the case of the input estimate
x 1

clause.title: Simultaneous measurement of resistance and reactance

clause.p: The resistance R R and reactance X X of a circuit element are determined by measuring the amplitude V V of a sinusoidally-alternating potential difference across its terminals, the amplitude I I of the alternating current passing through it, and the phase angle phi of the alternating potential difference relative to the alternating current. The input quantities are V V, I I and ϕ phi and the output quantities are the three impedance components R R, X X and Z Z, where Z 2 = R 2 + X 2 Z^2 = R^2 + X^2.
The application of Ohm’s law gives the trivariate measurement model

stem.asciimath: R = V/I cos phi, " " " " X = V/I sin phi, " " " " Z = V/I.

For reasons of simplicity, any systematic errors that may be present in V V, I I and ϕ phi are ignored.
In the GUM, reactance is denoted by X X, the notation also used here. The reactance X X, a component of the vector output quantity Y bb Y, is not to be confused with X bb X, the vector input quantity.

In the GUM, the data consists of a series of five sets of simultaneous indication values obtained independently (sets 1 to 5 of ). At least six sets are required for the covariance matrix in to be defined. Set 6 in is therefore included, and is chosen to be the average of sets 1 to 5. This particular choice for set 6 is not essential, but has the consequence that its inclusion does not change the average of the series.

clause.p: The determination of an estimate y bb y of Y bb Y and the associated covariance matrix U y bb(U_y) is considered. The approach here treats the problem defined by the measurement model and information about the input quantities in that model in the form of the sets x 1 , … , x n x_1, ..., x_n of indication values obtained independently given in . The GUM addresses this problem using the GUM uncertainty framework [ H.2.3]. The treatment here is extended to include MCM and an approach involving a multivariate t-distribution (see ).

For the purpose of applying the (generalized) GUM uncertainty framework, estimates x ≡ ( V ^ , I ^ , ϕ ^ ) T bb x -= (hat(V), hat(I), hat(phi))^{sf(T)} of the input quantities X ≡ ( V , I , ϕ ) T X -= (V, I, phi)^{sf(T)} are determined as the averages of the indication values given in [ 4.2].

The results obtained from the application of MCM and the alternative application of the GUM uncertainty framework are negligibly different, which indicates that the measurement functions in the measurement model can be linearized, to a good degree of approximation, about the estimates of the input quantities.

In this example, the conditions for the application of the Welch-Satterthwaite formula [ (G.2b)] of the GUM uncertainty framework to calculate an effective degrees of freedom are not valid because there are quantities for which the standard uncertainties have associated degrees of freedom that are finite and those quantities are not independent.

clause.title: Measurement of Celsius temperature using a resistance thermometer
General
clause.p: The example described in this subclause is concerned with the measurement of Celsius temperature by comparing the resistance of an industrial platinum resistance thermometer with that of a standard resistor using a resistance bridge. The measurement of a single Celsius temperature is described by a univariate measurement model (see ), and that of several temperatures by a multivariate measurement model (see ). The example illustrates the treatment of these (univariate and multivariate) measurement models using the (generalized) GUM uncertainty framework.

clause.title: Measurement of a single Celsius temperature

clause.p: The Celsius temperature θ theta is measured by comparing the resistance R ( θ ) R(theta) of a resistance thermometer with the resistance R S R_S of a standard resistor using a resistance bridge. In the temperature interval from 0 °C to 30 °C, the resistance of the thermometer is approximated by a parabolic function of its Celsius temperature θ theta.

where R 0 R_0, A A and B B are determined from a calibration of the thermometer. Estimates of R 0 R_0, A A and B B and the associated standard uncertainties are given in , and the non-zero correlation coefficients associated with pairs of the estimates in .

table.name: Estimates of the input quantities X ≡ ( R 0 , A , B , R S , r ) T bb X -= (R_0, A, B, R_S, r)^{sf(T)} for the measurement of a single Celsius temperature and the associated standard uncertainties (, and )
Non-zero correlation coefficients associated with pairs of estimates of the input quantities for the measurement of a single Celsius temperature (, and )

clause.p: The estimate of R S R_{"S"} and the associated standard uncertainty, determined by calibration, are given in . R S R_{"S"} is independent of the parameters R 0 R_0, A A and B B.
The quantity measured with the resistance bridge is the resistance ratio

The measured value of r r and the associated standard uncertainty are given in . The resistance ratio is independent of the parameters R 0 R_0, A A and B B of the resistance thermometer and the resistance R S R_{"S"} of the standard resistor; there are thus no further non-zero correlation coefficients beyond those given in .
By combining , the following measurement model for the Celsius temperature θ theta is obtained.

note.p: By considering expressions for the roots of a quadratic equation, transformation of the measurement model to explicit form is possible. However, the numerical evaluation of the transformed model can suffer from subtractive cancellation, and the evaluation of sensitivity coefficients is made more difficult by the transformation.

The resistance thermometer, standard resistor and resistance bridge described in are used to measure the resistance ratios r 1 , … , r 10 r_{1}, ..., r_{10} generated by ten Celsius temperatures θ 1 , … , θ 10 theta_{1}, ..., theta_{10}.

The estimates of the input quantities, R 0 , A , B R_0, A, B and R S R_{"S"} and the associated standard uncertainties are given in and the estimates of r 1 , … , r 10 r_{1}, ..., r_{10} in . The only non-zero correlation coefficients associated with pairs of the estimates remain those given in . The resistance ratios are assumed to be independent, an assumption that is valid if the magnitudes of the random errors in the measured resistance ratios dominate.

The standard uncertainties associated with the estimates of the Celsius temperatures and the correlation coefficients associated with pairs of estimates, derived from the matrix U y bb(U_y), are given in and , respectively.

The results given in and show how the standard uncertainty

This effect is due to the fact that the resistance thermometer was calibrated at the temperatures 0 °C, 15 °C, 20 °C and 25 °C, and that the Celsius temperature 0 °C during calibration was generated using an ice bath with a standard uncertainty three times smaller than those associated with the other three temperatures, which were generated using an oil bath.

Using the results given in the final column of , shows how the correlation coefficient associated with the pair of estimates θ ^ j hat(theta)_j and θ ^ 10 hat(theta)_{10} of Celsius temperatures θ j theta_j and θ 10 = 20 °C theta_{10} = 20 " °C" varies with θ ^ j hat(theta)_j, j = 1 , … , 9 j = 1, ..., 9. The correlation coefficient has a maximum for θ ^ j = θ ^ 6 hat(theta)_j = hat(theta)_6 and approaches zero as the absolute difference | θ ^ j − θ ^ 10 | |hat(theta)_j - hat(theta)_{10}| becomes large. The example demonstrates that quantities measured with the same instrument can be highly correlated.

annex.title: Evaluation of sensitivity coefficients and covariance matrix for multivariate measurement modelscovariance matrix measurement modelmultivariate sensitivity coefficient

clause.p: When recognized concepts from numerical linear algebra are used, a numerically stable way to form U y bb(U_y), the solution of the linear system of is as follows.

li.p: form the Cholesky factor R x bb(R_x) of U x bb(U_x), that is the upper triangular matrix such that R x T R x = U x bb(R_x)^{sf(T)} bb(R_x) = bb(U_x);
factorize C x bb(C_x) as the product C x = Q x W x bb(C_x) = bb(Q_x) bb(W_x), where Q x bb(Q_x) is an orthogonal matrix and W x bb(W_x) is upper triangular,
factorize C y bb(C_y) as the product C y = L y W y bb(C_y) = bb(L_y) bb(W_y), where L y bb(L_y) is lower triangular and W y bb(W_y) is upper triangular;
solve the matrix equation W y T M 1 = I bb(W_y)^{sf(T)} bb M_1 = bb I for M 1 bb M_1,
solve L y T M 2 = M 1 bb(L_y)^{sf(T)} bb M_2 = bb M_1 for M 2 bb M_2,
form M 3 = Q x T bb M_3 = Q_x^{sf(T)} M 2 bb M_2;
form K = W x T bb K = W_x^{sf(T)} M 3 bb M_3;
form M = R x K bb M = bb(R_x K);
orthogonally triangularize M bb M to give the upper triangular matrix R bb R,
form U y = R T R bb(U_y) = bb(R)^{sf(T)} bb(R).

clause.p: This procedure can be verified using elementary matrix algebra. Further details are available .

clause.title: General
clause.p: This annex is concerned with some details of the co-ordinate system transformation problem (see ). Subclause provides the joint PDF for Y bb Y analytically for the case that X 1 X_1 is described by the Gaussian distribution N ( x 1 , u x 2 ) N(x_1, u_x^2) and X 2 X_2 by N ( x 2 , u x 2 ) N(x_2, u_x^2), with X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 mutually independent. Subclause applies the GUM uncertainty framework for correlated and uncorrelated input quantities.

clause.title: Application of the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework

clause.p: For the co-ordinate system transformation problem considered in , the measurement model can be written as the bivariate measurement model

where it is understood that Y 1 ≥ 0 Y_1 >= 0 and − π < Y 2 ≤ π -pi < Y_2 <= pi. The input quantities X 1 X_1 and X 2 X_2 have estimates x 1 x_1 and x 2 x_2 and associated uncertainties u ( x 1 ) u(x_1) and u ( x 2 ) u(x_2), respectively, and associated covariance u ( x 1 , x 2 ) u(x_1, x_2).
The application of gives
as the estimates of Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2.
The sensitivity matrix C x C_x of dimension 2 × 2 2 xx 2 is given by evaluating
at X 1 = x 1 X_1 = x_1 and X 2 = x 2 X_2 = x_2. Therefore, provided y 1 = x 1 2 + x 2 2 > 0 y_1 = sqrt(x_1^2 + x_2^2) > 0,
The application of gives
as the covariance matrix associated with the estimates y = ( y 1 , y 2 ) T bb y = (y_1, y_2)^{sf(T)}, with u ( y 2 , y 1 ) = u ( y 1 , y 2 ) u(y_2, y_1) = u(y_1, y_2) and

In the context of the GUM uncertainty framework, Y bb Y is characterized by the bivariate Gaussian distribution N ( y , U y ) N(bb y,bb(U_y)), which can be used as the basis of determining coverage regions for Y bb Y corresponding to a prescribed coverage probability p p (see ).
Consider the case that u ( x 1 ) = u ( x 2 ) = u x u(x_1) = u(x_2) = u_x and u ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 u(x_1, x_2) = 0 (see ). Then,

In contrast, in the analytical treatment , where Y bb Y is not characterized by a bivariate Gaussian distribution, the conditions u ( x 1 ) = u ( x 2 ) u(x_1) = u(x_2) and u ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 u(x_1, x_2) = 0 are not sufficient for Y 1 Y_1 and Y 2 Y_2 to be independent. An additional condition, viz. x 1 = x 2 = 0 x_1 = x_2 = 0, is required (see ).

references.title: Normative referencesInternational vocabulary of metrology (VIM) Introduction to the GUM Supplement 1 to the GUM
references.p: The following referenced documents are indispensable for the application of this document. For dated references, only the edition cited applies. For undated references, the latest edition of the referenced document (including any amendments) applies.
